EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms resulted in widespread severe weather and flash flooding over eastern New York on Wednesday September 15, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree reportedly caught on fire due to a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread southeast New York during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8 inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Central Park recorded 7.19 inches of rain, including 3.15 inches in a single hour between approximately 9:00-10:00pm. This resulted in widespread catastrophic flash flooding across New York City that resulted in extensive road and subway closures and numerous water rescues. Additional rainfall totals from around the region include 7.38 inches at a New York Mesonet station in Fordham in the Bronx, a report of 7.00 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer in Boerum Hill, Brooklyn, 6.90 inches from the ASOS at LaGuardia Airport, a spotter report of 6.18 inches in Monroe, a spotter report of 8.09 inches in Scarsdale, and a CoCoRaHS report of 6.72 inches in Locust Valley. Ultimately 17 people died as a result of the flash flooding, including 13 in New York City and 4 in the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The body of a man in his 40s was found floating in the Gowanus Canal near 25th Street and Third Avenue in Greenwood Heights, Brooklyn on Thursday, September 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lifting warm front across the region brought a hot and humid air mass into the region, leading to maximum heat indices reaching 95 to 98 degrees across some valley locations of eastern New York.||An approaching cold front from the west led to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe with several reports of downed trees and power lines. A couple trees fell onto homes with another falling onto a camper trailer. No injuries were reported. A funnel cloud was spotted by an observer in Warren County looking northward toward Lake George. A lightning strike hit a structure near Galway, causing a fire. Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and wires were downed near the Town of New Paltz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across northern NY during the afternoon of March 1st delivering arctic air on strong winds during the overnight of March 1st-2nd. Persistent northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 40-45 mph began around 8 pm March 1st and continued until daybreak March 2nd. These strong winds combined with ambient temperatures of 5 below to 5 above zero created wind chills in the 15 to 30 below zero range. Power outages across the region were only a few thousand.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts in the 40-45 mph range were observed, including 48 mph at Plattsburgh Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low crossed our region around midday on November 28. An accompanying|mesolow formed over the eastern end of Lake Erie, which brought a brief burst of snow to downtown Buffalo and the airport. To the south of this low, lake effect snow began in earnest across Chautauqua County early in the afternoon on November 28. Inversions rose to a very high 13,000 feet by late afternoon. There also were weaker boundary layer winds, which resulted in a longer residence time over the lake and therefore increased moisture and instability flux into the lake effect bands. After the mesolow moved onshore, two lake effect bands developed, both of which were intense enough to support thundersnow. The northern band resulted in the greatest amounts, which also coincided with the heaviest amounts from the previous lake effect event. In this band, localized storm totals around a foot and a half were reported. Lake effect diminished early on November 29 as inversion heights fell off. Selected snow totals for this event included 10.2 inches in Jamestown, 9 inches in Findley Lake, and 17 inches in Cattaraugus.

Surface low pressure developed along a frontal boundary and moved northeast across SE New York, western southern New England and central New England on the 21st. Precipitation developed in the early morning and mostly in the form of snow or mixed snow and rain, then as the intensity of the precipitation increased during the late morning and afternoon, fell primarily as wet snow. Snowfall amounts across much of northern NY were 2 to 5 inches. Roads remained primarily wet, thus limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were generally 2 to 5 inches with no impacts, including roads as they stayed wet.

The ASOS at Central Park recorded 7.19 inches of rain, including 3.15 inches in a single hour between approximately 9:00-10:00pm. This resulted in widespread catastrophic flash flooding across New York City that resulted in extensive road and subway closures and numerous water rescues. Additional rainfall totals from around the region include 7.38 inches at a New York Mesonet station in Fordham in the Bronx, a report of 7.00 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer in Boerum Hill, Brooklyn, 6.90 inches from the ASOS at LaGuardia Airport, a spotter report of 6.18 inches in Monroe, a spotter report of 8.09 inches in Scarsdale, and a CoCoRaHS report of 6.72 inches in Locust Valley. Ultimately 17 people died as a result of the flash flooding, including 13 in New York City and 4 in the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters were up to the hoods of cars on Glen Avenue and Haseco Avenue in Port Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure developed along a frontal boundary and moved northeast across SE New York, western southern New England and central New England on the 21st. Precipitation developed in the early morning and mostly in the form of snow or mixed snow and rain, then as the intensity of the precipitation increased during the late morning and afternoon, fell primarily as wet snow. Snowfall amounts across much of northern NY were 2 to 5 inches. Roads remained primarily wet, thus limited impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were generally 2 to 4 inches with no impacts, including roads as they stayed wet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Hurricane Ida overspread southeast New York during the evening of September 1 and continued through the early morning hours of September 2. Rainfall totals ranged from 5-8 inches across much of the region, with much of that rain falling in just a few hours. The ASOS at Central Park recorded 7.19 inches of rain, including 3.15 inches in a single hour between approximately 9:00-10:00pm. This resulted in widespread catastrophic flash flooding across New York City that resulted in extensive road and subway closures and numerous water rescues. Additional rainfall totals from around the region include 7.38 inches at a New York Mesonet station in Fordham in the Bronx, a report of 7.00 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer in Boerum Hill, Brooklyn, 6.90 inches from the ASOS at LaGuardia Airport, a spotter report of 6.18 inches in Monroe, a spotter report of 8.09 inches in Scarsdale, and a CoCoRaHS report of 6.72 inches in Locust Valley. Ultimately 17 people died as a result of the flash flooding, including 13 in New York City and 4 in the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The basement of a home on Thorne Place in Rye was flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of low pressure systems brought a mix of rain and high elevation snow to eastern New York beginning the evening of Thursday, April 15th and ending during the early afternoon of Friday, April 16th, 2021. The majority of the heavy, wet snow fell across higher elevations (above 1500 feet), including the southern Adirondacks and eastern Catskills throughout this period, with a mix of rain and snow in the lower elevations. Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches in the higher elevations.||Key impacts: tree damage, power outages.

Ultimately 17 people died as a result of the flash flooding, including 13 in New York City and 4 in the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Victory Boulevard was closed near Richmond Avenue in the Bulls Head section of Staten Island due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Ohio Valley and Middle Atlantic on Sunday, January 31, 2021. The low then consolidated along the Middle Atlantic coast early on Monday, February 1, 2021 and intensified as it slowly tracked to the north-northeast. The low meandered slowly north as it moved along the northeast and New England coast later on Monday February 1, 2021 through Wednesday, February 3, 2021. The low brought a major winter storm to Southeast New York, with the most significant impacts occurring on Monday, February 1, 2021.  3 to 6 inches of snow had already accumulated across the New York City metro by the start of the morning commute. Near blizzard conditions then occurred through the rest of the morning into the early afternoon with snowfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour. Strong winds combined with these heavy snow rates to produce visibilities one quarter mile or less at times. The heavier bands of snow lifted north across the rest of southeast New York in the afternoon. Some warmer air moved over portions of New York City and Long Island late on Monday, February 1, 2021, which change the snow to sleet and even some light rain early Tuesday, February 2, 2021. Colder air returned however later Tuesday, February 2, 2021, changing all lingering precipitation to light snow. Light snow bands lingered across some of Southeast New York through the early morning hours on February 3, 2021. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches across eastern Long Island, with the rest of southeast New York receiving 12 to 18 inches of snow. Portions of the Lower Hudson Valley observed 20 inches of snow with some locations observing around two feet. Winds gusted 40 to 55 mph at times on Monday, February 1, 2021, which caused power outages. Travel was also severely impacted as many flights were cancelled  and travel by train was suspended. New York City issued a Winter Weather Emergency Declaration restricting all non-emergency travel on Monday, February 1, 2021. COVID testing and vaccination sites were closed. Impacts were limited on February 2, 2021 and February 3, 2021 as the snow was largely light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters, COOP Observer, and Fire Department/Rescue reported 18 to 23 inches of snow. A trained spotter 2 miles north of Orange Lake reported 22.3 inches of snow. A COOP observer in Port Jervis reported 22 inches of snow. Fire Department/Rescue reported 20.4 inches of snow in Middletown. The Montgomery Airport ASOS measured a 39 mph wind gust at 4:10 pm and then again at 11:11 pm on February 1, 2021.

Strong winds combined with these heavy snow rates to produce visibilities one quarter mile or less at times. The heavier bands of snow lifted north across the rest of southeast New York in the afternoon. Some warmer air moved over portions of New York City and Long Island late on Monday, February 1, 2021, which change the snow to sleet and even some light rain early Tuesday, February 2, 2021. Colder air returned however later Tuesday, February 2, 2021, changing all lingering precipitation to light snow. Light snow bands lingered across some of Southeast New York through the early morning hours on February 3, 2021. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches across eastern Long Island, with the rest of southeast New York receiving 12 to 18 inches of snow. Portions of the Lower Hudson Valley observed 20 inches of snow with some locations observing around two feet. Winds gusted 40 to 55 mph at times on Monday, February 1, 2021, which caused power outages. Travel was also severely impacted as many flights were cancelled  and travel by train was suspended. New York City issued a Winter Weather Emergency Declaration restricting all non-emergency travel on Monday, February 1, 2021. COVID testing and vaccination sites were closed. Impacts were limited on February 2, 2021 and February 3, 2021 as the snow was largely light.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported 10 to 15 inches of snow. The Wantagh New York State Mesonet Station measured a 50 mph wind gust at 6:05 pm on February 1, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration wintry weather event occurred on Thursday, February 18, 2021 through Friday, February 19, 2021. A frontal wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast on February 18, 2021. The frontal wave combined with a strong upper level to produce a brief period of moderate to heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon on Thursday, February 18, 2021. The snow also mixed with sleet at times during the late morning and early afternoon hours across portions of the New York City metro and Long Island. Snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour occurred with locally 2 inches per hour at times. The precipitation became light and changed to spotty freezing rain and freezing drizzle during the late afternoon through the overnight hours on February 18, 2021. The overall weather pattern did not change to much on Friday, February 19, 2021. Weak waves of low pressure continued to track along the frontal zone off the Middle Atlantic coast, combining with the lingering strong jet stream aloft to produce periods of mainly light snow. The light snow persisted into the early evening hours before tapering off just before midnight on Friday, February 19, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported 3 to 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough dug across the nation's midsection with an outbreak of Arctic air from the Great Plains to Texas. Low pressure developed near Louisiana and tracked across Pennsylvania toward southern New England on the eastern fringe of the cold air mass. A weaker initial wave of precipitation produced 1-3 inches of snow with a lull before the main event with deeper moisture, isentropic lift, and favorable jet dynamics arrived later in the day on February 15. While most of the area was originally forecast to see heavy snow, large scale drying aloft encroached from the south. This resulted in far less snow over the Southern Tier and from the Genesee Valley eastward. Selected snow totals for this event included 8.5 inches in Kenmore, 13 inches in Batavia, 12 inches in Hilton, 12 inches in Lewiston, 10 inches in Albion, and 7.5 inches in Warsaw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stacked coastal storm threw Atlantic moisture back across western and north central New York Tuesday (Groundhog Day) and into Wednesday. Strong mesoscale banding occurred over the North Country and on the western edge of the mid-upper level low. Later, banding within the shield of synoptic snow enhanced snowfall rates to over an inch an hour for sites Rochester and eastward with total snowfall approaching 10 -12 inches in some areas. The Rochester evening commute was slow with snow covered roads. Selected snow totals during the event included 11.7 inches in Watertown, 12 inches on Croghan, 15 inches in Lacona, 9.5 inches in Williamson, 10 inches in Fairport, and 10 inches in Geneva.

Strong cold advection behind a front pushed much colder air aloft over the area. However, as it did so, winds remained very southwesterly as the initial lake effect bands formed. This allowed the first Lake Erie bands to develop over the Niagara Peninsula before crossing the Niagara River and swinging rapidly across Niagara and Erie counties before sitting a while and eventually advancing back northward toward the City of Buffalo. Off Lake Ontario, the initial bands formed over the Thousand Islands region below shifting southward. The heaviest snow rates observed generally ranged on the order of 1 to 2 inches per hour. Luckily, the bands were transient, so while snow totals were heavy, nowhere saw extreme accumulations. Selected snow totals included 15 inches in Hooker, 7 inches in Orchard Park, 13.2 inches in Pembroke, 8.3 inches in Attica, 10.2 inches in Watertown, 10 inches in Lancaster, and 7.4 inches at the Buffalo Niagara International Airport.

A localized band of heavy lake effect snow developed in northern and central Oswego County during the evening of February 7, then drifted a little south overnight with the heaviest snow focusing on the central portion of the county.  The lake effect snow also impacted the far northern end of Cayuga County. This was an event with a strong land breeze component that forced an east-west band of strong convergence just off the southern shore of Lake Ontario. The eastern extension of this band clipped far northern Cayuga and west-central Oswego counties as the shoreline starts to curve northward in that area.  Thermodynamic profiles were strongly supportive of large dendrites with a favorable layer of dendritic growth beneath the inversion which resulted in rapid accumulation of snow even with low liquid water content within the snow. Selected snow totals included 12 inches in New Haven and 9.5 inches in Mexico.

Light snow occurred over eastern New York during the morning and afternoon of the 22nd. The Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga region picked up three to eight inches, and the Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills saw three to five inches. The snow resulted in slippery travel conditions and some school closures. Key impacts: school closures.

Another period of westerly flow developed late in the evening on 2/9 and continued through 2/10 in the wake of a weak trough. Lake induced equilibrium levels rose and favored dendritic growth zone beneath the inversion yielding high snow to liquid water ratios. Similar to the last event, this was another case of relatively light winds and cold air allowing for a land breeze dominated band structure with the heaviest snow close to the lakeshore. Selected snow totals included 19.6 inches on the campus of SUNY-Oswego and 12 inches in Altmar.

A winter storm swept across the Northeastern US and brought a wintry mix to Central New York and Northeast Pennsylvania. A few counties received up to a half inch of ice. Snowfall generally ranged from around an inch to around 6 inches, along with a coating of ice. The counties that received the most ice had scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated 0.25 to 0.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing storm system, a cold and moist northwest flow of air crossed the relatively warm waters of Lake Ontario. This brought heavy lake effect snow from the evening of the 19th through the evening of the 20th to portions of the Finger Lakes region, especially Onondaga and southern Cayuga counties. During the afternoon of the 20th, a lake effect band broke off and developed into a snow squall, causing multiple accidents and cars to drive off the road on Interstate 81 near Tully, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated up to 13 inches. A lake effect band broke off during the afternoon of the 20th and developed into a snow squall. This caused multiple car accidents and cars to drive off the road along Interstate 81 in the Tully, NY area and caused a complete closure of the Interstate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed through Central New York on the 22nd. This brought a quick shot of snow across most of Central New York. The most snow fell in northern Oneida county, where it lasted into the evening. The rest of the region was limited to a few inches of snow from a quick burst that occurred in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 7 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across northern NY during the afternoon of March 26th. This was the same system responsible for the tornado outbreak across AL/MS/GA the previous day. Strong upper atmospheric winds (energy) combined with unseasonably mild and humid air accounted for a line of thunderstorms to develop just east of Lake Ontario and move ENE across the Adirondacks into the southern Champlain Valley. One particular thunderstorm in this line caused scattered wind damage from northern Lewis and northern Hamilton counties into the Minerva-Schroon Lake-Ticonderoga-Westport area. Damage was largely in the form of numerous trees and subsequent downed utility lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed by thunderstorm winds on White Hill road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down onto Ford Brook North Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lifting warm front across the region brought a hot and humid air mass into the region, leading to maximum heat indices reaching 95 to 98 degrees across some valley locations of eastern New York.||An approaching cold front from the west led to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe with several reports of downed trees and power lines. A couple trees fell onto homes with another falling onto a camper trailer. No injuries were reported. A funnel cloud was spotted by an observer in Warren County looking northward toward Lake George. A lightning strike hit a structure near Galway, causing a fire. Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.

Thunderstorms developed along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary and pushed inland from Chautauqua County toward Wyoming County. These cells pulsed up and down a few times and displayed lightning jumps at times. This resulted in sporadic wind damage. A second cluster of cells moved inland from Lake Erie overnight. These cells results in marginally severe wind reports that with wind gust reports being well represented by the New York State Mesonet observations. Shear was rapidly increasing as these cells were moving in, too, so the expectation was that they would grow stronger as they moved into the area, which is what occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed at Farnsworth Road and Witherden.

These cells pulsed up and down a few times and displayed lightning jumps at times. This resulted in sporadic wind damage. A second cluster of cells moved inland from Lake Erie overnight. These cells results in marginally severe wind reports that with wind gust reports being well represented by the New York State Mesonet observations. Shear was rapidly increasing as these cells were moving in, too, so the expectation was that they would grow stronger as they moved into the area, which is what occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed along Route 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lifting warm front across the region brought a hot and humid air mass into the region, leading to maximum heat indices reaching 95 to 98 degrees across some valley locations of eastern New York.||An approaching cold front from the west led to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe with several reports of downed trees and power lines. A couple trees fell onto homes with another falling onto a camper trailer. No injuries were reported. A funnel cloud was spotted by an observer in Warren County looking northward toward Lake George. A lightning strike hit a structure near Galway, causing a fire. Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.

A hot and humid airmass developed over eastern New York on the 27th and persisted through the 30th. Heat indices of 95 to 105 degrees were common, with the highest values on the 28th and 29th. The high reached the 90s for all four days at Poughkeepsie, and three days at Albany and Glens Falls.

A couple of rounds of thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 30th across eastern New York. Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. Microbursts were confirmed in Wappingers Falls and Canaan, where estimated winds of 75 to 85 miles per hour downed numerous trees in a localized area. The storms knocked out power to more than 4,000 customers in the Mid-Hudson Valley, and also delayed travel on the Metro North's Hudson line due to trees falling on the tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on South Road and Anthony Drive.

A cold front dropped southward across the region and interacted with modestly moist and unstable conditions. A humid boundary layer was in place as evidenced by surface dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s. All of this worked to generate widespread thunderstorms mid-afternoon into the early evening over the Southern Tier. A cold front came through in pieces though with first a wind shift to the north noted as early as the early afternoon over Southern Tier, however lower dewpoints failed to arrive until late evening and overnight. Strong low level lapse rates were present, however shear was weak and the mean layer flow was only around 5 knots. The one hour flash flood guidance was about 1.5 inches across the Southern Tier. The storms that developed were very slow movers and flash flooding was observed in all three southern Tier counties. Storms that had stronger cores aloft produced isolated downbursts in southeastern Chautauqua County and southeastern Allegany County. Of interest, peak wind gusts with convection came from the east, which is a non-typical wind direction. This likely resulted in more wind damage to trees than normal for these cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Main Street Extension closed from Bacon Road to the Pennsylvania state line due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on Brantingham Road.

A passing upper-level disturbance amid a warm and humid air mass led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. Some of these storms became severe, producing wind damage across parts of Dutchess County. Between 1.50 and 2.50 inches of rain fell in some areas which led to urban flooding in parts of Downtown Albany. A lightning strike caused a house fire in Ulster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a house fire near Clintondale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass developed over eastern New York on the 27th and persisted through the 30th. Heat indices of 95 to 105 degrees were common, with the highest values on the 28th and 29th. The high reached the 90s for all four days at Poughkeepsie, and three days at Albany and Glens Falls.

A hot and humid airmass developed over eastern New York on the 27th and persisted through the 30th. Heat indices of 95 to 105 degrees were common, with the highest values on the 28th and 29th. The high reached the 90s for all four days at Poughkeepsie, and three days at Albany and Glens Falls.

A cold front dropped southward across the region and interacted with modestly moist and unstable conditions. A humid boundary layer was in place as evidenced by surface dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s. All of this worked to generate widespread thunderstorms mid-afternoon into the early evening over the Southern Tier. A cold front came through in pieces though with first a wind shift to the north noted as early as the early afternoon over Southern Tier, however lower dewpoints failed to arrive until late evening and overnight. Strong low level lapse rates were present, however shear was weak and the mean layer flow was only around 5 knots. The one hour flash flood guidance was about 1.5 inches across the Southern Tier. The storms that developed were very slow movers and flash flooding was observed in all three southern Tier counties. Storms that had stronger cores aloft produced isolated downbursts in southeastern Chautauqua County and southeastern Allegany County. Of interest, peak wind gusts with convection came from the east, which is a non-typical wind direction. This likely resulted in more wind damage to trees than normal for these cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 219 flooded and closed in front of the Dollar General.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Harrisville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across northern NY during the afternoon of March 26th. This was the same system responsible for the tornado outbreak across AL/MS/GA the previous day. Strong upper atmospheric winds (energy) combined with unseasonably mild and humid air accounted for a line of thunderstorms to develop just east of Lake Ontario and move ENE across the Adirondacks into the southern Champlain Valley. One particular thunderstorm in this line caused scattered wind damage from northern Lewis and northern Hamilton counties into the Minerva-Schroon Lake-Ticonderoga-Westport area. Damage was largely in the form of numerous trees and subsequent downed utility lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of trees and wires downed by thunderstorm winds between Minerva and Schroon Lake along State Route 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved across northern NY during the afternoon of March 1st delivering arctic air on strong winds during the overnight of March 1st-2nd. Persistent northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts in excess of 40-45 mph began around 8 pm March 1st and continued until daybreak March 2nd. These strong winds combined with ambient temperatures of 5 below to 5 above zero created wind chills in the 15 to 30 below zero range. Power outages across the region were only a few thousand.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts in the 35-40 mph range were observed.

Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. The storms knocked out power to more than 4,000 customers in the Mid-Hudson Valley, and also delayed travel on the Metro North's Hudson line due to trees falling on the tracks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave combined with a surface trough resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley during the early afternoon hours. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with weak steering flow and high precipitable water values, resulting in several reports of flash flooding. Based on upper air soundings from Upton, NY, precipitable water values increased from around 1.75 inches on the morning of June 8th to just over 2 inches that evening. Rainfall amounts varied widely across the area, with a COOP observer in Port Jervis reporting 1.85 inches of rain and the ASOS at the White Plains Airport recording 1.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The basement of a home at 19 Conklingtown Road in Chester was reported flooded. Radar estimated rainfall amounts in the area were greater than 3 inches over the previous hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave combined with a surface trough resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley during the early afternoon hours. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with weak steering flow and high precipitable water values, resulting in several reports of flash flooding. Based on upper air soundings from Upton, NY, precipitable water values increased from around 1.75 inches on the morning of June 8th to just over 2 inches that evening. Rainfall amounts varied widely across the area, with a COOP observer in Port Jervis reporting 1.85 inches of rain and the ASOS at the White Plains Airport recording 1.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taconic State Parkway in Hawthorne was closed in both directions in the vicinity of Stevens Avenue due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southward across the region and interacted with modestly moist and unstable conditions. A humid boundary layer was in place as evidenced by surface dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s. All of this worked to generate widespread thunderstorms mid-afternoon into the early evening over the Southern Tier. A cold front came through in pieces though with first a wind shift to the north noted as early as the early afternoon over Southern Tier, however lower dewpoints failed to arrive until late evening and overnight. Strong low level lapse rates were present, however shear was weak and the mean layer flow was only around 5 knots. The one hour flash flood guidance was about 1.5 inches across the Southern Tier. The storms that developed were very slow movers and flash flooding was observed in all three southern Tier counties. Storms that had stronger cores aloft produced isolated downbursts in southeastern Chautauqua County and southeastern Allegany County. Of interest, peak wind gusts with convection came from the east, which is a non-typical wind direction. This likely resulted in more wind damage to trees than normal for these cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto Garfield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto Blue Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 30th across eastern New York. Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. Microbursts were confirmed in Wappingers Falls and Canaan, where estimated winds of 75 to 85 miles per hour downed numerous trees in a localized area. The storms knocked out power to more than 4,000 customers in the Mid-Hudson Valley, and also delayed travel on the Metro North's Hudson line due to trees falling on the tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey confirmed a series of microbursts between Canaan, NY and the New York/Massachusetts border. Damage began near the intersection of Route 22 and Beebe Hill Road, just south of Route 295. The Canaan Department of Public Works reported approximately 50 trees down in a remote wooded area. A newspaper reported a tree down on a car with damage to the windshield and hood. Seven roads were closed due to downed trees and wires. The width of the damage varied from 10 yards to up to a mile wide. The damage continued across the border to Richmond, MA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with warm and humid conditions, which then triggered scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Many storms became severe and produced damaging winds as well as 1 inch hail. While fallen trees and utility wires made up most of the damage, there was also some minor structural damage as well. One storm became tornadic as a weak, short-lived tornado passed near Dryden in Thompkins County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees on roads and one onto a house.

Many of these storms had damaging winds, knocking down trees and wires across Central New York. Some structural damage was also reported including window damage at a New York government building. A few of these storms also produced large hail, 1 inch or large.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across northern NY during the afternoon of March 26th. This was the same system responsible for the tornado outbreak across AL/MS/GA the previous day. Strong upper atmospheric winds (energy) combined with unseasonably mild and humid air accounted for a line of thunderstorms to develop just east of Lake Ontario and move ENE across the Adirondacks into the southern Champlain Valley. One particular thunderstorm in this line caused scattered wind damage from northern Lewis and northern Hamilton counties into the Minerva-Schroon Lake-Ticonderoga-Westport area. Damage was largely in the form of numerous trees and subsequent downed utility lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed by thunderstorm winds along Route 74 near Paradox Lake, east of River road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 30th across eastern New York. Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. Microbursts were confirmed in Wappingers Falls and Canaan, where estimated winds of 75 to 85 miles per hour downed numerous trees in a localized area. The storms knocked out power to more than 4,000 customers in the Mid-Hudson Valley, and also delayed travel on the Metro North's Hudson line due to trees falling on the tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed 0.5 miles south of Ballard Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary and pushed inland from Chautauqua County toward Wyoming County. These cells pulsed up and down a few times and displayed lightning jumps at times. This resulted in sporadic wind damage. A second cluster of cells moved inland from Lake Erie overnight. These cells results in marginally severe wind reports that with wind gust reports being well represented by the New York State Mesonet observations. Shear was rapidly increasing as these cells were moving in, too, so the expectation was that they would grow stronger as they moved into the area, which is what occurred.

Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down onto Fishers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level ridge remained off the east coast of the United States, allowing southerly flow to stream north from the Gulf of Mexico, across the Mississippi Valley, and into the lower Great Lakes region. Temperatures in the Southern Tier were in the mid 80s with dewpoints near 70 degrees.  Shear aloft was minimal, but there was plenty of instability. Overall, convection was expected to be sub-severe and initiating on lake breeze boundaries. This was the case until one cell grew rapidly in the warm and unstable environment over the Southern Tier, necessitating a warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Leather Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough passed through Central New York, triggering scattered severe thunderstorms along the boundary. The winds associated with these storms blew down trees, snapped utility poles, and blew a roof off of a trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds snapped power poles, knocking down powerlines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down tree branches around Syracuse University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with warm and humid conditions, which then triggered scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Many storms became severe and produced damaging winds as well as 1 inch hail. While fallen trees and utility wires made up most of the damage, there was also some minor structural damage as well. One storm became tornadic as a weak, short-lived tornado passed near Dryden in Thompkins County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lifting warm front across the region brought a hot and humid air mass into the region, leading to maximum heat indices reaching 95 to 98 degrees across some valley locations of eastern New York.||An approaching cold front from the west led to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe with several reports of downed trees and power lines. A couple trees fell onto homes with another falling onto a camper trailer. No injuries were reported. A funnel cloud was spotted by an observer in Warren County looking northward toward Lake George. A lightning strike hit a structure near Galway, causing a fire. Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.

Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed at Moffitt Beach near Route 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 30th across eastern New York. Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. Microbursts were confirmed in Wappingers Falls and Canaan, where estimated winds of 75 to 85 miles per hour downed numerous trees in a localized area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary and pushed inland from Chautauqua County toward Wyoming County. These cells pulsed up and down a few times and displayed lightning jumps at times. This resulted in sporadic wind damage. A second cluster of cells moved inland from Lake Erie overnight. These cells results in marginally severe wind reports that with wind gust reports being well represented by the New York State Mesonet observations. Shear was rapidly increasing as these cells were moving in, too, so the expectation was that they would grow stronger as they moved into the area, which is what occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level ridge remained off the east coast of the United States, allowing southerly flow to stream north from the Gulf of Mexico, across the Mississippi Valley, and into the lower Great Lakes region. Temperatures in the Southern Tier were in the mid 80s with dewpoints near 70 degrees.  Shear aloft was minimal, but there was plenty of instability. Overall, convection was expected to be sub-severe and initiating on lake breeze boundaries. This was the case until one cell grew rapidly in the warm and unstable environment over the Southern Tier, necessitating a warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating with temperatures in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in sufficient destabilization for storms to form, specifically along lake breeze boundaries in the middle to late afternoon. A Heat Advisory was in effect for lower elevations excluding Jefferson County. Surface based instability was strong and precipitable water values were almost 2 inches. Shear was minimal across most of the region with 20-25 knots across western New York increasing to 30-35 knots across Lake Ontario and the North Country. The strongest shear was across southern Ontario, north of Lake Ontario, where widespread convection was occurring by early afternoon. A few storms were able to capitalize on lake breeze boundaries to the south of the Buffalo Metro area with isolated damage in southern Erie County and Wyoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees were downed onto Route 20A between Route 19 and Route 238.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with warm and humid conditions, which then triggered scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Many storms became severe and produced damaging winds as well as 1 inch hail. While fallen trees and utility wires made up most of the damage, there was also some minor structural damage as well. One storm became tornadic as a weak, short-lived tornado passed near Dryden in Thompkins County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires blocking a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through Central New York which triggered isolated thunderstorms, some of which became severe. One storm produced a tornado that took the roof off of a barn and moved the barn off of its foundation. The tornado also knocked down several trees as it passed through a yard and into a state forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near a barn off of Sulphur Springs Road. The tornado tore down several trees in multiple directions along a visible path. Then it lifted off the roof of the barn with the structure collapsing on itself. Additional damage included the barn shifting off of it's foundation. Trees were also snapped high up. The tornado proceeded through West Branch State Forest with intermediate damage before lifting as it reached Harris Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure across the western Atlantic Ocean advected in a hot and humid air mass into the region on Monday, June 7, 2021. The combination of heat and humidity led to heat index values peaking around 95 degrees for areas mainly along the Hudson River during the afternoon hours.

A passing upper-level disturbance amid a warm and humid air mass led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. Some of these storms became severe, producing wind damage across parts of Dutchess County. Between 1.50 and 2.50 inches of rain fell in some areas which led to urban flooding in parts of Downtown Albany. A lightning strike caused a house fire in Ulster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near Wingdale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree limb was downed on Route 23B near Route 9H, taking down lines and damaging a porch roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped southward across the region and interacted with modestly moist and unstable conditions. A humid boundary layer was in place as evidenced by surface dewpoints in the upper 60s to lower 70s. All of this worked to generate widespread thunderstorms mid-afternoon into the early evening over the Southern Tier. A cold front came through in pieces though with first a wind shift to the north noted as early as the early afternoon over Southern Tier, however lower dewpoints failed to arrive until late evening and overnight. Strong low level lapse rates were present, however shear was weak and the mean layer flow was only around 5 knots. The one hour flash flood guidance was about 1.5 inches across the Southern Tier. The storms that developed were very slow movers and flash flooding was observed in all three southern Tier counties. Storms that had stronger cores aloft produced isolated downbursts in southeastern Chautauqua County and southeastern Allegany County. Of interest, peak wind gusts with convection came from the east, which is a non-typical wind direction. This likely resulted in more wind damage to trees than normal for these cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State route 305 was closed due to water flowing over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the Lake Erie lake breeze boundary and pushed inland from Chautauqua County toward Wyoming County. These cells pulsed up and down a few times and displayed lightning jumps at times. This resulted in sporadic wind damage. A second cluster of cells moved inland from Lake Erie overnight. These cells results in marginally severe wind reports that with wind gust reports being well represented by the New York State Mesonet observations. Shear was rapidly increasing as these cells were moving in, too, so the expectation was that they would grow stronger as they moved into the area, which is what occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were reported down along Route 5 in West Bloomfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of the area was in the warm sector dry slot during the morning hours.  Storms developed along a pre-frontal trough around midday and moved east across the area. The axis of greatest instability as the pre-frontal trough moved through the region was the Southern Tier to Northern Finger Lakes and North Country. The lowest severe weather risk was across the Niagara Frontier including the Buffalo metro area due to lake shadowing. Precipitable water values increased to near 2 inches, with dew points approaching 70 degrees ahead of the storms. This also made the risk for heavy rainfall in any thunderstorms a concern. Storms did produce high rainfall rates, but they were moving at a fast enough pace that the overall flooding threat was limited. There were a few discrete cells that formed east of Lake Ontario around midday that produced some wind damage in Lewis County. Meanwhile, the main line of storms associated with the pre-frontal trough initiated along a line stretching from Lake Ontario southwest across Lake Erie into eastern Ohio. This line of storms just took off from there with bowing segments and some supercellular development occurring basically across the entire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house near Highway 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating with temperatures in the 90s and dewpoints in the 70s resulted in sufficient destabilization for storms to form, specifically along lake breeze boundaries in the middle to late afternoon. A Heat Advisory was in effect for lower elevations excluding Jefferson County. Surface based instability was strong and precipitable water values were almost 2 inches. Shear was minimal across most of the region with 20-25 knots across western New York increasing to 30-35 knots across Lake Ontario and the North Country. The strongest shear was across southern Ontario, north of Lake Ontario, where widespread convection was occurring by early afternoon. A few storms were able to capitalize on lake breeze boundaries to the south of the Buffalo Metro area with isolated damage in southern Erie County and Wyoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front collided with warm and humid conditions, which then triggered scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon across Central New York. One storm produced a weak, short-lived tornado. This tornado knocked down several trees. It also went through the yard of a residence, knocking a small shed off of its foundation as well as damaging a portion of their fence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Yellow Barn State Forest, east of Yellow Barn Road. It continued to travel eastward and passed over Card Road. It then struck a farm on Irish Settlement Road, snapping and uprooting multiple trees. Then it continued eastward and crossed through a wooded section of Beam Hill Road. The tornado then struck the yard of a house on the west side of Route 38, causing significant tree damage to the yard, a shed to blow off its foundation, and a blow-down of a fence. The tornado then crossed Route 38 just south of Dryden, before lifting just east of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lifting warm front across the region brought a hot and humid air mass into the region, leading to maximum heat indices reaching 95 to 98 degrees across some valley locations of eastern New York.||An approaching cold front from the west led to numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe with several reports of downed trees and power lines. A couple trees fell onto homes with another falling onto a camper trailer. No injuries were reported. A funnel cloud was spotted by an observer in Warren County looking northward toward Lake George. A lightning strike hit a structure near Galway, causing a fire. Sporadic power outages occurred as a result of these storms with more than 1,000 National Grid customers without power in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in the Town of Old Forge. All lanes on Route 28 were blocked between Crosby Road and Adams Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of thunderstorms occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 30th across eastern New York. Scattered reports of wind damage occurred with the strongest storms. Microbursts were confirmed in Wappingers Falls and Canaan, where estimated winds of 75 to 85 miles per hour downed numerous trees in a localized area. The storms knocked out power to more than 4,000 customers in the Mid-Hudson Valley, and also delayed travel on the Metro North's Hudson line due to trees falling on the tracks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Frankfort.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced microbursts around Wappingers Falls. Two main areas of damage were noted. The first was at Bowdoin Park along the Hudson River where numerous trees were downed. The second was near Mesier Park in the village of Wappingers Falls where approximately 50 trees were downed with some falling on homes. At least one power pole was snapped. The length of the damage was approximately 1.5 miles, with a width of approximately 275 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a light to moderate snowfall to eastern New York from the morning hours of Tuesday, January 26th into the morning of Wednesday, January 27th, 2021. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 in the mid-Hudson Valley up to about 11 in southern Hamilton county. Some of the steadier snowfall impacted the Tuesday evening commute.

A combination of an arctic airmass and gusty winds resulted in dangerously cold wind chills across much of eastern New York the night of January 28th into January 29th, 2021. Low temperatures ranged from 10 degrees below zero in the southern Adirondacks up to 10 degrees above zero in the mid-Hudson Valley. Wind chill values ranged from 28 degrees below zero in the southern Adirondacks up to 3 degrees above zero in the mid-Hudson Valley.

A strong cold front moved across the region on Monday, March 1 advecting in a much colder air mass along with a prolonged period of strong to locally damaging winds which continued into the early part of Tuesday, March 2. Wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were common across the region, which led to reports of downed trees and power lines, resulting in scattered power outages. The more concentrated areas without power included around the Capital District and into Ulster County in the mid-Hudson Valley.||As colder air moved into the region, a few snow showers and squalls passed across the region during the evening hours of March 1. At the Albany International Airport, visibility dropped to one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 46 mph within a snow squall, which lasted for about 17 minutes.||Colder air continued to filter into the region during the overnight and early morning hours of March 2 with low temperatures mainly in the single digits and teens, but below zero in the typical colder spots in the higher elevations. This, combined with continued gusty winds, led to wind chill values falling to between 15 and 24 degrees below zero in some areas.||Key Impacts: tree damage, power outages, road closures, transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51 mph wind gust was recorded at the New York State Mesonet at Schaghticoke. A large 40-foot tree was downed across a roadway in East Greenbush.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 7 westbound in the city of Schenectady had the right lane closed at Golf Road due to a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front brought a period of strong winds to the region spanning the evening hours on Friday, March 12 into the early morning hours on Saturday, March 13. Wind gusts reached 45 to 55 mph in some areas. Some downed trees and wires occurred as a result of the wind. One tree fell onto two unoccupied vehicles in Saugerties, causing extensive damage.||Key Impacts: tree damage, damage to vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 32 at Rowe Road was closed due to downed trees and wires near Feura Bush. A peak wind gust of 54 mph was recorded at the Albany International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered showers and thunderstorms across the area. An upstream elevated mixed layer and associated mesoscale convective vortex allowed for convection to cross from lower Michigan, across southern Ontario, and into western New York by late in the evening. Sufficient deep layer shear was present to support continued severe activity as the storms approached our area. A quasi-linear convective system resulted as the activity moved through, resulting in widespread wind damage reports and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported downed onto a powerline.

Wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were common across the region, which led to reports of downed trees and power lines, resulting in scattered power outages. The more concentrated areas without power included around the Capital District and into Ulster County in the mid-Hudson Valley.||As colder air moved into the region, a few snow showers and squalls passed across the region during the evening hours of March 1. At the Albany International Airport, visibility dropped to one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 46 mph within a snow squall, which lasted for about 17 minutes.||Colder air continued to filter into the region during the overnight and early morning hours of March 2 with low temperatures mainly in the single digits and teens, but below zero in the typical colder spots in the higher elevations. This, combined with continued gusty winds, led to wind chill values falling to between 15 and 24 degrees below zero in some areas.||Key Impacts: tree damage, power outages, road closures, transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 47 mph wind gust was recorded at an observing site in Cobleskill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front brought a period of strong winds to the region spanning the evening hours on Friday, March 12 into the early morning hours on Saturday, March 13. Wind gusts reached 45 to 55 mph in some areas. Some downed trees and wires occurred as a result of the wind. One tree fell onto two unoccupied vehicles in Saugerties, causing extensive damage.||Key Impacts: tree damage, damage to vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree came down onto two unoccupied vehicles in a driveway on Hommelville Road in Saugerties. Both vehicles sustained extensive damage according to a local police chief. Other trees and wires were downed across the area.

Weak low pressure moved along a cold front that moved across NY/VT during November 26th. Rain and rain showers transitioned into a wet snow beginning at higher elevations around noon and eventually into the Champlain Valley by early evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from a wet few niches in the Champlain Valley with 4 to 8 inches in the some of the higher elevations in the Adirondacks. The wet nature of the snow led to scattered power outages in the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow by early evening with snowfall amounts ranging from a dusting to 2 inches in the valley with 3 to 8 inches in higher elevations of the Adirondacks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow by early afternoon with snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches in higher elevations of the Adirondacks. These higher amounts led to isolated power outages due to the wet nature of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated upper level low positioned over southern Canada allowed for persistent westerly flow into the southern Adirondacks. Pieces of upper energy moving through the flow resulted in a period of moderate to heavy lake effect snow from the evening of Thursday, January 21st through the morning of Friday, January 22nd, 2021. Snowfall amounts between 6 and 12 inches fell across portions of Northern Herkimer and Hamilton counties.

A combination of an arctic airmass and winds resulted in low wind chills across much of eastern New York the night of January 29th into January 30th, 2021. Low temperatures ranged from 18 degrees below zero in the southern Adirondacks up to 11 degrees above zero in the mid-Hudson Valley. Wind chill values ranged from 25 degrees below zero up to 8 degrees above zero.

Snowfall totals ranged from 2 in the mid-Hudson Valley up to about 11 in southern Hamilton county. Some of the steadier snowfall impacted the Tuesday evening commute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure moved along a cold front that moved across NY/VT during November 26th. Rain and rain showers transitioned into a wet snow beginning at higher elevations around noon and eventually into the Champlain Valley by early evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from a wet few niches in the Champlain Valley with 4 to 8 inches in the some of the higher elevations in the Adirondacks. The wet nature of the snow led to scattered power outages in the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow by late afternoon with snowfall amounts ranging from 2 to 5 inches  in the valley with 4 to 8 inches in higher elevations of the Adirondacks. These higher amounts led to isolated power outages due to the wet nature of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered showers and thunderstorms across the area. An upstream elevated mixed layer and associated mesoscale convective vortex allowed for convection to cross from lower Michigan, across southern Ontario, and into western New York by late in the evening. Sufficient deep layer shear was present to support continued severe activity as the storms approached our area. A quasi-linear convective system resulted as the activity moved through, resulting in widespread wind damage reports and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over several streets with a depth up to the bottom of the car doors in some spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and weak surface cold front crossed the eastern Great Lakes Monday morning. A line of thunderstorms over southern Ontario moved across Lake Erie and eventually reached the western end of Lake Ontario around daybreak. It then moved down the length of Lake Ontario and into the eastern Lake Ontario region. Surface based instability was mainly confined close to the lake shores, which was an influence of the warm lake waters warming the boundary layer. This resulted in a few strong wind gusts along the shores of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario as the line passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and powerlines were reported to be down via social media.

While snowfall was well below normal for the season, there were still several inches of liquid equivalent in the snowpack on the Tug Hill coming into the end of March. This rapidly melted with a brief and abrupt warm up toward the end of the month, resulting in a rapid but short-lived rise on the Black River in Watertown on March 28-29. The Black River crested at 10.1 feet, which is just slightly above the flood stage of 10.0 feet.

Snowfall amounts ranged from a wet few niches in the Champlain Valley with 4 to 8 inches in the some of the higher elevations in the Adirondacks. The wet nature of the snow led to scattered power outages in the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow by late afternoon with snowfall amounts ranging from 1 to 2 inches  in the valley with 2 to 4 inches in higher elevations closer to the Adirondacks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic storm system brought a variety of weather to eastern New York on Wednesday, April 21st, 2021, including a tornado, severe thunderstorms and high elevation snow. An EF-1 tornado briefly touched down in Amenia, NY (Dutchess County), causing damage to several houses and ripping off the roof of a restaurant. Additional reports of downed trees and power lines were reported south of the Capital District. North of the Capital District, there was light accumulations of snowfall, ranging from a tenth of an inch up to 4 inches in Hamilton County.||Key Impacts: damage to trees and power lines, power outages, structural damage, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down just south of Amenia, New York |and moved north northeast into the village. The tornado started|near the intersection of Powder House Road and Ohandley Drive and|caused sporadic damage along its path length. One house along|Ohandley drive had partial roof damage and a two by four and a|small branch driven into the side of the home. Nine out of ten |homes on Ohandley Drive sustained damage. The tornado ended near |the intersection of Mechanic Street and East Main Street where it |ripped the roof off a restaurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an arctic airmass and winds resulted in low wind chills across much of eastern New York the night of January 29th into January 30th, 2021. Low temperatures ranged from 18 degrees below zero in the southern Adirondacks up to 11 degrees above zero in the mid-Hudson Valley. Wind chill values ranged from 25 degrees below zero up to 8 degrees above zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex long duration winter storm system affected Central New York from late in the day of January 31st through around midday February 3rd. A strong surface low pressure system developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast during this time and brought moderate to heavy snow to portions of Central New York. As the storm pulled away, wrap-around moisture brought additional lake-enhanced snow to portions of the area. ||Note that the snow amounts indicated in the event descriptions are storm totals for the entire storm which stretched into February. This entry will be continued in February's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 13 to 21 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm in the Great Lakes on January 15th, transferred energy to a developing coastal low of the NJ coast during the morning of the 16th. Precipitation began late evening on the 15th across NY with rain in the valleys and wet snow in the higher elevations. During the early morning hours and through midday on January 16th, rain changed to a heavy, wet snow that accumulated 4 to 6 inches in the valleys with 8 to 12 inches in higher elevations. The weight of the heavy wet snow led to scattered power outages across northern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain mixed with snow in the early morning hours changed to a heavy wet snow and accumulated from 3 to 6 inches in the valleys to up to 8 inches in the elevated terrain. The weight of the snow led to isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stacked low pressure near Indiana slowly moved the area during the period. A surface low redeveloped along the coast and tracked across New England. Initially, most precipitation was rain in our area, but it gradually changed over to snow. The snow began as synoptic snow before transitioning to lake effect as colder air advected into the region. Selected snow totals for the event included 12 inches in Redfield, 12 inches in Barnes Corners, 14 inches in Highmarket, and 14 inches in Lowville.

A mid-level shortwave trough dove through the Great Lakes and to the south of Lake Erie that returned deep moisture over Lake Erie. This combined with marginal lake surface to 850 hPa temperature differences generated lake effect snow. The boundary layer flow in the lake effect mixed layer started west-southwest and shifted to more of a west-northwest flow during the event as a surface trough passed through. Throughout this event, lake-induced equilibrium levels only rose to 7-8 kft. However, as the air mass became colder, more of the lake-induced convective layer moved into the dendritic growth layer,  yielding fluffier snow. Lake effect snows also developed east of Lake Ontario later in the event. In this case, the lake-induced equilibrium rose to 10 kft yielding heavy snow for Jefferson and Lewis counties, focusing on the Tug Hill. Selected snow totals included 24.5 inches in Forestville, 21.5 inches in Silver Creek, 35.5 inches in Springville, 31.5 inches in Glenwood, 29.5 inches in Perrysburg, 19 inches in Java Center, 22.3 inches in Warsaw, 14.6 inches in Worth, 18.1 inches in Highmarket, and 20 inches in Redfield.

An upper level disturbance ahead of a strong cold front moved into a marginally unstable airmass to trigger scattered thunderstorms across the southern Adirondacks and central NY. One thunderstorm briefly intensified to produce localized damaging winds near Schroon Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on wires along Old Schroon road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure developed off the Middle Atlantic coast during the morning of January 3, 2021. The low then tracked north-northeast away from the Middle Atlantic. but south and east of Long Island during the late day on January 3, 2021. The low then moved away from the coast during the early morning hours on January 4, 2021. The system brought a light snow accumulation across the interior of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 3.9 inches of snow in Newburgh, 3.3 inches of snow 1 mile north of Orange Lake, and 3.0 inches of snow in Gardnertown. CoCoRaHS reported 3.1 inches of snow 1 mile south of Walden.

Lake effect snows also developed east of Lake Ontario later in the event. In this case, the lake-induced equilibrium rose to 10 kft yielding heavy snow for Jefferson and Lewis counties, focusing on the Tug Hill. Selected snow totals included 24.5 inches in Forestville, 21.5 inches in Silver Creek, 35.5 inches in Springville, 31.5 inches in Glenwood, 29.5 inches in Perrysburg, 19 inches in Java Center, 22.3 inches in Warsaw, 14.6 inches in Worth, 18.1 inches in Highmarket, and 20 inches in Redfield.

Weakening low pressure system passed to our north across Ontario and Quebec. This provided large scale moisture that when combined with pre-frontal temps aloft just cold enough to support lake effect resulted in snow east of Lake Ontario. Upslope initially aided the combined synoptic moisture and lake-induced instability to generate heavy snow in the Tug Hill region, however as the event continued, flow eventually turned more northwesterly in the wake of the system. This resulted in heavy snow slinking southward in the Monroe County to Cayuga County shoreline areas. Selected snow totals included 17 inches in Constableville, 11 inches in Mannsville, 11 inches in Lacona, 10 inches in Gates, 9 inches in Webster, 9 inches in Cato, and 10 inches in Marion.

High winds occurred over the region as high pressure built in from the west behind a strengthening coastal low pressure system heading northeast after passing well to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at Baiting Hollow at 1029 AM local standard time along with a measured sustained wind of 51 mph recorded 714 AM local time. Both measurements were at an elevation of 78 feet above ground. Additionally, a measured sustained wind of 44 mph was recorded at Great Gull Island at 554 AM local standard time at an elevation of 53 feet above ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm in the Great Lakes on January 15th, transferred energy to a developing coastal low of the NJ coast during the morning of the 16th. Precipitation began late evening on the 15th across NY with rain in the valleys and wet snow in the higher elevations. During the early morning hours and through midday on January 16th, rain changed to a heavy, wet snow that accumulated 4 to 6 inches in the valleys with 8 to 12 inches in higher elevations. The weight of the heavy wet snow led to scattered power outages across northern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain mixed with snow in the early morning hours changed to a heavy wet snow that accumulated several inches.

A surface low pressure system tracked from the Ohio Valley to western New York on the 26th. This combined with an upper level shortwave moving across the southern Great Lakes. The combination of these two features brought a period of snow to Central New York from the afternoon of the 26th into the early morning hours of the 27th. The highest snowfall amounts were found in northern Oneida County, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 6 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded frontal system tracked from the Ohio Valley to Central New York during the afternoon of the 15th into the morning of the 16th. Meanwhile, a secondary low pressure system developed off the southern New England coast during the morning of the 16th and tracked northeastward up the coast throughout the day on the 16th. The combination of these two systems brought an initial batch of snow to Central New York during the morning of the 16th. Then, as the coastal low pulled farther northeastward towards the Canadian Maritimes, wrap-around moisture coming off of Lake Ontario created lake enhanced snow that persisted from late in the day on the 16th through the 17th. The highest snowfall totals were found across northern Oneida County, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 7-14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed to the northwest of a slow-moving coastal low on February 1st. The snow was light initially during the morning hours of the 1st, but an area of heavy snow expanded northwestward during the afternoon and evening, with snowfall rates of one to three inches per hour observed over areas mainly south of Albany. The heavier snow pushed through the Capital District and toward points north and west overnight into the 2nd while dissipating over areas south of the Capital District.  Storm totals ranged from one to two feet over the Mid-Hudson Valley, eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Schoharie Valley. Amounts were generally 6 to 12 inches over the Capital District, Mohawk Valley, and southern Adirondacks, with lesser amounts of 2 to 6 inches over the Lake George-Saratoga region. The heavy snowfall prompted a tractor trailer ban along I-84, and truck and speed restrictions along the New York State Thruway south of Albany. Metro North train service was also suspended. Numerous schools were closed, and states of emergency were issued by some counties and municipalities. Key impacts: transportation delays, government closures, school closures.

Light snow moved into portions of eastern New York during the late evening of the 15th, and turned to freezing rain through the night before ending around sunrise on the 16th. Flat ice accretion of a tenth to a half an inch impacted most of eastern New York aside from the Taconics and southern Adirondacks, while one to four inches of snow was reported along and north of I-90. The freezing rain resulted in icy roads with crashes reported along the New York State Thruway and other highways throughout the region. Scattered power outages also occurred, and many schools were closed or delayed. Key impacts: traffic accidents, power outages, damage to trees, school closures.
