A stacked coastal storm threw Atlantic moisture back across western and north central New York Tuesday (Groundhog Day) and into Wednesday. Strong mesoscale banding occurred over the North Country and on the western edge of the mid-upper level low. Later, banding within the shield of synoptic snow enhanced snowfall rates to over an inch an hour for sites Rochester and eastward with total snowfall approaching 10 -12 inches in some areas. The Rochester evening commute was slow with snow covered roads. Selected snow totals during the event included 11.7 inches in Watertown, 12 inches on Croghan, 15 inches in Lacona, 9.5 inches in Williamson, 10 inches in Fairport, and 10 inches in Geneva.

A strong upper level disturbance and its associated frontal system a brief period of moderate to locally heavy snow. The snow quickly transitioned to plain rain during the late afternoon and early evening as temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported 3.8 inches of snow in Chestnut Ridge. A trained spotter in Stony Point reported 2.5 inches of snow.

Low pressure developed off the NC/VA coast during the morning on Sunday, February 7, 2021. The low then tracked northeast away from the Middle Atlantic through the afternoon as it intensified, then well south and east of Long Island in the late afternoon and evening. A band of moderate to heavy snow developed across southern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley during the late morning hours. and the continued moving east across portions of the New York City metro and Long Island through the rest of afternoon. The initial moderate to heavy snow band merged with a second band developing across eastern Long Island in the afternoon. Snowfall rates within the bands were around one to as high as two inches per hour at times. Surrounding this moderate to heavy band was a steady snowfall through much of the day. Snowfall accumulation across much of Southern Connecticut ranged from 3 to as high as 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile northeast of Riverdale reported 7.0 inches of snow. The public reported 4 to 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive mid-level trough passed across the eastern Great Lakes. Wrap around moisture increased across the area, and cold air advection sent temperatures plummeting. Lake effect snow ramped up southeast of Lake Ontario and Lake Erie in the west-northwest flow as inversion heights climbed. As the event unfolded, satellite imagery displayed a moisture connection to Lake Huron for the Lake Erie band, but not as deep a connection to the partially frozen Georgian Bay for the Lake Ontario band. This resulted in very impressive totals downstream from Lake Erie but less impressive totals downstream of Lake Ontario. Selected snow totals for the event included 32 inches in Boston, 30.6 inches in West Falls, 10.8 inches in Silver Creek, 30.8 inches near Warsaw, 9.5 inches in Pultneyville, 8 inches in Ashford Hollow, and 8.3 inches in South Butler.

Total ice accumulations up to 0.10 occurred in these counties.

This resulted in very impressive totals downstream from Lake Erie but less impressive totals downstream of Lake Ontario. Selected snow totals for the event included 32 inches in Boston, 30.6 inches in West Falls, 10.8 inches in Silver Creek, 30.8 inches near Warsaw, 9.5 inches in Pultneyville, 8 inches in Ashford Hollow, and 8.3 inches in South Butler.

Moderate to heavy snow developed to the northwest of a slow-moving coastal low on February 1st. The snow was light initially during the morning hours of the 1st, but an area of heavy snow expanded northwestward during the afternoon and evening, with snowfall rates of one to three inches per hour observed over areas mainly south of Albany. The heavier snow pushed through the Capital District and toward points north and west overnight into the 2nd while dissipating over areas south of the Capital District.  Storm totals ranged from one to two feet over the Mid-Hudson Valley, eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Schoharie Valley. Amounts were generally 6 to 12 inches over the Capital District, Mohawk Valley, and southern Adirondacks, with lesser amounts of 2 to 6 inches over the Lake George-Saratoga region. The heavy snowfall prompted a tractor trailer ban along I-84, and truck and speed restrictions along the New York State Thruway south of Albany. Metro North train service was also suspended. Numerous schools were closed, and states of emergency were issued by some counties and municipalities. Key impacts: transportation delays, government closures, school closures.

Low pressure developed off the NC/VA coast during the morning on Sunday, February 7, 2021. The low then tracked northeast away from the Middle Atlantic through the afternoon as it intensified, then well south and east of Long Island in the late afternoon and evening. A band of moderate to heavy snow developed across southern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley during the late morning hours. and the continued moving east across portions of the New York City metro and Long Island through the rest of afternoon. The initial moderate to heavy snow band merged with a second band developing across eastern Long Island in the afternoon. Snowfall rates within the bands were around one to as high as two inches per hour at times. Surrounding this moderate to heavy band was a steady snowfall through much of the day. Snowfall accumulation across much of Southern Connecticut ranged from 3 to as high as 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and Broadcast media reported 4 to 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cross country storm system, responsible for heavy snows in Seattle, snow, ice and report cold in the southern Plains and the Tennessee valley moved into New England during the overnight hours of February 15-16th. ||Snow overspread northern New York around midnight on the 16th, mixing with sleet and freezing rain at times around sunrise before ending by mid-morning. Combined snow and sleet accumulations were generally 3 to 6 inches with minimal icing from freezing rain. However, many roads were treacherous due to the wintry mix and many schools cancelled classes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 3 to 6 inches were common along with a brief period of sleet and freezing rain.

The same storm that brought 6+ inches of rainfall and flash flooding to California as well as 6+ feet to the Sierra Nevada late the previous week moved into the Great Lakes on January 31st and February 1st. This system joined with another system to develop a coastal low off the NJ coast during the night of February 1st and slowly moved across the Gulf of Maine on February 2nd.  A Nor'easter with snowfall of 18 inches in NYC and more than 2 feet in parts of Northern NJ/Eastern PA during the 31st and 1st slowly spread snow northward into northern NY.||Snowfall developed just after midnight on February 2nd across northern New York with a snow band remaining nearly stationary near the Canadian border through midday. As secondary band of snow, with snowfall rates of 1-2+ inches an hour moved into the northern Champlain Valley of NY during the later afternoon and early evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches in the southern Champlain Valley, and southern St. Lawrence valley to 12-18 inches in the northern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 12 inches in the valley of eastern Clinton with 10 to 18 inches in the slopes of western Clinton county.

Showers and thunderstorms developed over northern Cattaraugus and southern Erie counties along the Lake Erie lake breeze. Storms tracked east-northeast across Wyoming County and into Livingston County where more instability was present. There was a fairly quick increase in lightning along with a building core aloft that preceded the reports of severe damage. Divergence aloft on radar indicated the core was building and suggested continued growth from that point. The severe storm was short-lived before rapidly weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in Livonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved east across the eastern Great Lakes after midday. Showers and cloud cover close to Lake Erie and Lake Ontario combined with gusty southwest winds off the lake to keep areas northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario stable. More sun and no lake influence allowed for moderate destabilization from the western Southern Tier through the Finger Lakes to points southeast of Lake Ontario. Scattered storms developed from the western Southern Tier through the Finger Lakes and Genesee Valley and slowly grew upscale.  A cluster of storms in Wayne County slowly grew upscale into a short segment that started to show better velocity signatures and a bowing structure as it moved east across the county.  This line of storms produced numerous wind damage reports from eastern Wayne County to Oswego and Southern Lewis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

Light snow occurred over eastern New York during the morning and afternoon of the 22nd. The Mohawk Valley, southern Adirondacks, and Lake George Saratoga region picked up three to eight inches, and the Mid-Hudson Valley and eastern Catskills saw three to five inches. The snow resulted in slippery travel conditions and some school closures. Key impacts: school closures.

A pre-frontal trough moved east across the eastern Great Lakes after midday. Showers and cloud cover close to Lake Erie and Lake Ontario combined with gusty southwest winds off the lake to keep areas northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario stable. More sun and no lake influence allowed for moderate destabilization from the western Southern Tier through the Finger Lakes to points southeast of Lake Ontario. Scattered storms developed from the western Southern Tier through the Finger Lakes and Genesee Valley and slowly grew upscale.  A cluster of storms in Wayne County slowly grew upscale into a short segment that started to show better velocity signatures and a bowing structure as it moved east across the county.  This line of storms produced numerous wind damage reports from eastern Wayne County to Oswego and Southern Lewis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed onto Merritt Road in Granby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same storm that brought 6+ inches of rainfall and flash flooding to California as well as 6+ feet to the Sierra Nevada late the previous week moved into the Great Lakes on January 31st and February 1st. This system joined with another system to develop a coastal low off the NJ coast during the night of February 1st and slowly moved across the Gulf of Maine on February 2nd.  A Nor'easter with snowfall of 18 inches in NYC and more than 2 feet in parts of Northern NJ/Eastern PA during the 31st and 1st slowly spread snow northward into northern NY.||Snowfall developed just after midnight on February 2nd across northern New York with a snow band remaining nearly stationary near the Canadian border through midday. As secondary band of snow, with snowfall rates of 1-2+ inches an hour moved into the northern Champlain Valley of NY during the later afternoon and early evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches in the southern Champlain Valley, and southern St. Lawrence valley to 12-18 inches in the northern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged 10 to 18 inches across northern Franklin county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a wintry mix of precipitation to the region during the evening hours of Friday, January 1st into the morning of Saturday, January 2nd, 2021. Snowfall totals were minimal and ranged from about 0.5 inches up to 3 inches. Minor ice accumulations also occurred but were constrained to Dutchess, Montgomery and Ulster counties. Total ice accumulations up to 0.10 occurred in these counties.

Moderate to heavy snow developed to the northwest of a slow-moving coastal low on February 1st. The snow was light initially during the morning hours of the 1st, but an area of heavy snow expanded northwestward during the afternoon and evening, with snowfall rates of one to three inches per hour observed over areas mainly south of Albany. The heavier snow pushed through the Capital District and toward points north and west overnight into the 2nd while dissipating over areas south of the Capital District.  Storm totals ranged from one to two feet over the Mid-Hudson Valley, eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Schoharie Valley. Amounts were generally 6 to 12 inches over the Capital District, Mohawk Valley, and southern Adirondacks, with lesser amounts of 2 to 6 inches over the Lake George-Saratoga region. The heavy snowfall prompted a tractor trailer ban along I-84, and truck and speed restrictions along the New York State Thruway south of Albany. Metro North train service was also suspended. Numerous schools were closed, and states of emergency were issued by some counties and municipalities. Key impacts: transportation delays, government closures, school closures.

Snowfall totals ranged from only a few inches in eastern Hamilton county, up to 12 inches across northern Herkimer and northwest Hamilton counties.

Light snow moved into portions of eastern New York during the late evening of the 15th, and turned to freezing rain through the night before ending around sunrise on the 16th. Flat ice accretion of a tenth to a half an inch impacted most of eastern New York aside from the Taconics and southern Adirondacks, while one to four inches of snow was reported along and north of I-90. The freezing rain resulted in icy roads with crashes reported along the New York State Thruway and other highways throughout the region. Scattered power outages also occurred, and many schools were closed or delayed. Key impacts: traffic accidents, power outages, damage to trees, school closures.

Scattered storms developed from the western Southern Tier through the Finger Lakes and Genesee Valley and slowly grew upscale.  A cluster of storms in Wayne County slowly grew upscale into a short segment that started to show better velocity signatures and a bowing structure as it moved east across the county.  This line of storms produced numerous wind damage reports from eastern Wayne County to Oswego and Southern Lewis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure tracked from the Middle Atlantic and Ohio Valley on Monday, February 15, 2021 to over New England on February 16, 2021. Lingering low level cold air across interior Lower Hudson Valley allowed freezing rain to develop late February 15, 2021 into the early morning hours on February 16, 2021. The temperatures warmed above freezing quickly after sunrise, changing the precipitation to plain rain across Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montgomery Airport ASOS recorded 0.36 inches of freezing rain. Trained spotters reported 0.10 to 0.30 inches of freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and its associated frontal system a brief period of moderate to locally heavy snow. The snow quickly transitioned to plain rain during the late afternoon and early evening as temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 2 to 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance and its associated frontal system a brief period of moderate to locally heavy snow. The snow quickly transitioned to plain rain during the late afternoon and early evening as temperatures rose above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snow developed to the northwest of a slow-moving coastal low on February 1st. The snow was light initially during the morning hours of the 1st, but an area of heavy snow expanded northwestward during the afternoon and evening, with snowfall rates of one to three inches per hour observed over areas mainly south of Albany. The heavier snow pushed through the Capital District and toward points north and west overnight into the 2nd while dissipating over areas south of the Capital District.  Storm totals ranged from one to two feet over the Mid-Hudson Valley, eastern Catskills, Helderbergs, and Schoharie Valley. Amounts were generally 6 to 12 inches over the Capital District, Mohawk Valley, and southern Adirondacks, with lesser amounts of 2 to 6 inches over the Lake George-Saratoga region. The heavy snowfall prompted a tractor trailer ban along I-84, and truck and speed restrictions along the New York State Thruway south of Albany. Metro North train service was also suspended. Numerous schools were closed, and states of emergency were issued by some counties and municipalities. Key impacts: transportation delays, government closures, school closures.

Light snow moved into portions of eastern New York during the late evening of the 15th, and turned to freezing rain through the night before ending around sunrise on the 16th. Flat ice accretion of a tenth to a half an inch impacted most of eastern New York aside from the Taconics and southern Adirondacks, while one to four inches of snow was reported along and north of I-90. The freezing rain resulted in icy roads with crashes reported along the New York State Thruway and other highways throughout the region. Scattered power outages also occurred, and many schools were closed or delayed. Key impacts: traffic accidents, power outages, damage to trees, school closures.

The same storm that brought 6+ inches of rainfall and flash flooding to California as well as 6+ feet to the Sierra Nevada late the previous week moved into the Great Lakes on January 31st and February 1st. This system joined with another system to develop a coastal low off the NJ coast during the night of February 1st and slowly moved across the Gulf of Maine on February 2nd.  A Nor'easter with snowfall of 18 inches in NYC and more than 2 feet in parts of Northern NJ/Eastern PA during the 31st and 1st slowly spread snow northward into northern NY.||Snowfall developed just after midnight on February 2nd across northern New York with a snow band remaining nearly stationary near the Canadian border through midday. As secondary band of snow, with snowfall rates of 1-2+ inches an hour moved into the northern Champlain Valley of NY during the later afternoon and early evening. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches in the southern Champlain Valley, and southern St. Lawrence valley to 12-18 inches in the northern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 12 inches in the valley of eastern Clinton with 10 to 18 inches in the slopes of western Clinton county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance moving north along the periphery of a upper level area of high pressure in a very warm, unstable airmass led to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon and evening hours of July 13th. Two thunderstorms brought isolated wind damage to St. Lawrence county in the form of downed branches and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree, branches and utility lines downed by thunderstorm  winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms formed along a passing cold front after clear skies helped create an unstable environment. These storms caused damage throughout Central New York, including destroying a lumber storage building that was under construction. The collapse of this structure resulted in three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a lumber building under construction, resulting in 3 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and strong cold front moved into a warm, moist unstable airmass across northern NY during the afternoon of June 21st. Thunderstorms developed ahead of this cold front, with strong winds aloft, to help support locally severe storms with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local microburst in Fort Covington with reports of several trees uprooted or snapped and an old barn building collapsed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across eastern New York during the afternoon and evening of the 26th. There were several reports of trees and wires downed due to thunderstorm winds. Power outages totaled around 2,100 in Albany and Schenectady Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Beechvue Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient with high winds occurred as high pressure built into the region behind a strengthening low pressure system that was exiting Northern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple strong wind gusts occurred, including a gust to 79 mph at Stony Brook (measured at an elevation of 159 ft above ground.) at 234 AM Local Standard Time and gust to 75 mph at Eaton's Neck Lighthouse (measured at an elevation of 71 ft. above ground) at 204 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact closed low passed just to the northwest of the area. Accompanying this system, a strong and well-mixed low-level jet advanced just to the east of Lake Erie. This was an unusual system since it originated in the southern branch of the jet stream and was already a closed mid-level low. This did not fit the pattern of what typically causes strong winds in our area, but it certainly did cause very strong winds with lots of damage and numerous measured gusts over 60 mph. There seemed to be a favorable environment for funneling along the Chautauqua Ridge and across the Niagara Frontier, as those were the locations with the strongest large scale wind gusts. Large scale wind gusts behind the front also caused a seiche on Lake Erie. The water level at Buffalo peaked at 11.4 feet above low water datum. Selected non-thunderstorm measured wind gusts included 69 mph in Fredonia, 60 mph at Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 62 mph in Batavia, 59 mph at the Rochester Airport, and 58 mph in Warsaw. In addition, ahead of the cold front, early morning convection affect the Lake Erie shoreline as well as the Buffalo area, convection redeveloped in the afternoon over the North Country with discrete cells resulting in concentrated damage in Lewis County.

A passing shortwave combined with a surface trough resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across the Lower Hudson Valley during the early afternoon hours. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with weak steering flow and high precipitable water values, resulting in several reports of flash flooding. Based on upper air soundings from Upton, NY, precipitable water values increased from around 1.75 inches on the morning of June 8th to just over 2 inches that evening. Rainfall amounts varied widely across the area, with a COOP observer in Port Jervis reporting 1.85 inches of rain and the ASOS at the White Plains Airport recording 1.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yard and basement flooding reported via mPING along Hudson Street in Pelham Manor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sustained wind of 40 mph occurred at Stewart Airport at 245 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moved across the Northeast, dragging a cold front across southeastern New York with high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of high winds including sustained winds of 65 mph at Stony Brook (elevation of 159 feet above ground level), a gust of 64 mph and a sustained wind of 55 mph occurred at around 450 PM Local Standard Time at Eaton's Neck Lighthouse (elevation of 71 feet above ground level), and a gust of 67 mph along with a sustained wind of 55 mph at 756 PM Local Standard Time at Eaton's Neck Lighthouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the St. Lawrence River Valley on Friday, March 26, 2021. A warm front lifted northward across the region in the morning, advecting in a very warm air mass along with some non-severe showers and thunderstorms. High temperatures reached the 70s to near 80 in some locations with record highs being set at both the Albany and Poughkeepsie airports.||The system's cold front pushed eastward across the region during the midday hours, pushing a squall line through the Adirondacks. Damaging winds within the squall line downed some trees and power lines across Hamilton County.||In the wake of the cold front, a much cooler air mass filtered into the region which resulted in a period of strong winds. Occasional wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph were observed across the region, which downed additional trees and power lines.||Key Impacts: damage to trees and power lines, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large birch tree was uprooted on Brockley Drive at Grantwood Road near Delmar. A 55 mph wind gust was recorded at the New York State Mesonet at Voorheesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the region on Monday, March 1 advecting in a much colder air mass along with a prolonged period of strong to locally damaging winds which continued into the early part of Tuesday, March 2. Wind gusts between 40 and 60 mph were common across the region, which led to reports of downed trees and power lines, resulting in scattered power outages. The more concentrated areas without power included around the Capital District and into Ulster County in the mid-Hudson Valley.||As colder air moved into the region, a few snow showers and squalls passed across the region during the evening hours of March 1. At the Albany International Airport, visibility dropped to one quarter of a mile with a peak wind gust of 46 mph within a snow squall, which lasted for about 17 minutes.||Colder air continued to filter into the region during the overnight and early morning hours of March 2 with low temperatures mainly in the single digits and teens, but below zero in the typical colder spots in the higher elevations. This, combined with continued gusty winds, led to wind chill values falling to between 15 and 24 degrees below zero in some areas.||Key Impacts: tree damage, power outages, road closures, transportation delays.

East to southeast ocean swells of 4 to 6 ft likely resulted in rough surf and a high risk of rip currents Saturday Night into Sunday ahead of an approaching cold front. NWS New York, NY Surf Zone Forecast program begin until 5/27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recovery teams found the body of a woman in her sixties who went missing in the waters off Far Rockaway on May 22nd Saturday night. Police said a body was recovered May 23rd Sunday morning near the search site of a missing swimmer last seen the night before.||NYPD and FDNY search teams were dispatched Saturday evening to Far Rockaway after reports of a missing swimmer were made around 9:30 p.m.  The woman allegedly stripped off her clothes and ran into the Rockaway Inlet off Beach 17th St. and Seagirt Blvd. at about 9:00 p.m. According to some witnesses, they saw the woman struggling in the water 30 feet away from shore and screaming for help. Once first responders arrived to the incident the woman had already been pulled down and disappeared.||This was likely a rip current fatality due to the likely surf zone conditions and details of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact closed low passed just to the northwest of the area. Accompanying this system, a strong and well-mixed low-level jet advanced just to the east of Lake Erie. This was an unusual system since it originated in the southern branch of the jet stream and was already a closed mid-level low. This did not fit the pattern of what typically causes strong winds in our area, but it certainly did cause very strong winds with lots of damage and numerous measured gusts over 60 mph. There seemed to be a favorable environment for funneling along the Chautauqua Ridge and across the Niagara Frontier, as those were the locations with the strongest large scale wind gusts. Large scale wind gusts behind the front also caused a seiche on Lake Erie. The water level at Buffalo peaked at 11.4 feet above low water datum. Selected non-thunderstorm measured wind gusts included 69 mph in Fredonia, 60 mph at Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 62 mph in Batavia, 59 mph at the Rochester Airport, and 58 mph in Warsaw. In addition, ahead of the cold front, early morning convection affect the Lake Erie shoreline as well as the Buffalo area, convection redeveloped in the afternoon over the North Country with discrete cells resulting in concentrated damage in Lewis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was reported to a home on Route 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front brought a couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region, mainly along and north of Interstate 90. A few individual thunderstorms became severe across the Lake George Saratoga region during the early afternoon hours before the main line of thunderstorms progressed from west to east from the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley to the Hudson River during the late afternoon and early evening hours. There were a couple of reports of hail, but these storms brought mostly damaging winds with downed trees and power lines, a couple of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in a fire near West Glens Falls. Minor flooding occurred across southern Herkimer County, near Poland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed on wires, resulting in a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight risk for severe thunderstorms was outlooked for all of our area with very unstable conditions to the south of a warm front and ahead of a shortwave impulse lifting out of Ohio. CAPE values were running 1500-2000 J/kg, and shear values were running 20-25 knots in the west to 30-40 knots in the east. Wind damage and tropical downpours ended up the main hazards as wet bulb zero heights were over 14,000 feet. Severe thunderstorms were observed across much of the area and the adjoining Great Lakes waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

A few individual thunderstorms became severe across the Lake George Saratoga region during the early afternoon hours before the main line of thunderstorms progressed from west to east from the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley to the Hudson River during the late afternoon and early evening hours. There were a couple of reports of hail, but these storms brought mostly damaging winds with downed trees and power lines, a couple of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in a fire near West Glens Falls. Minor flooding occurred across southern Herkimer County, near Poland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in the Town of Petersburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A healthy upper level trough and frontal boundary moved from Ontario into northern NY during the evening and overnight hours of July 20th. Ahead of this system, a vigorous squall line of thunderstorms that developed in Ontario during the early afternoon hours moved across northern NY from mid-afternoon into the evening hours with numerous reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight risk for severe thunderstorms was outlooked for all of our area with very unstable conditions to the south of a warm front and ahead of a shortwave impulse lifting out of Ohio. CAPE values were running 1500-2000 J/kg, and shear values were running 20-25 knots in the west to 30-40 knots in the east. Wind damage and tropical downpours ended up the main hazards as wet bulb zero heights were over 14,000 feet. Severe thunderstorms were observed across much of the area and the adjoining Great Lakes waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed between 2 AM and 4 AM in an area of stronger convergence off Lake Erie in southern Erie County. Automated gauges from weather underground were helpful to get ground truth rainfall reports. Repeated heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in the Southern Tier. Multiple road closures were reported. Areal flooding lingered after the flash flood. The heaviest rain hit the headwaters of the east and west branches of Cazenovia Creek, resulting in very high flows into the early afternoon. The gage EBZN6 quickly rose over 6 feet to around 9 feet in just two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 5 was reported to be closed due to flooding from Harrington Road to Center Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced torrential rainfall across the Village of Fonda on Monday, July 19, 2021. Between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell in just a couple of hours. A small creek north of the village overflowed its banks, sending water and mud downstream into the village. Storm drains became plugged up with debris so water was unable to get through. Many roads were closed and impassable in and around the village until early Tuesday morning, July 20, 2021. ||A state of emergency was issued in the Village of Fonda as a result of the flooding.||In addition, this thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Johnstown, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat unstable air mass was in place ahead of a warm front that was being forced by a convectively-enhanced shortwave crossing southern Ontario.  This feature generated extensive strong to severe convection from Toronto to the Thousand Islands during the late afternoon and early evening. As the line of convection approached the Thousand Islands, the strongest cell in the line initiated a bow that supported a severe thunderstorm warning.  While there was little shear, downdraft CAPE was approaching 1000 J/kg, which likely tipped the scales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were downed over a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level trough crossed the eastern Great Lakes from late morning through mid afternoon, providing weak large scale ascent.  A very warm and moist air mass was in place with surface dewpoints in the lower 70s and temperatures warming into the 80s, contributing to moderate to strong instability.  Shear was very weak, with effective deep layer shear only 15-20 knots. The weakly forced and weakly sheared environment in the presence of strong instability supported a pulse storm mode, with rapidly developing cores which briefly became severe and then quickly collapsed. Initial convective development was mainly along lake breeze boundaries, with subsequent convection developing along eastward propagating outflow boundaries that crossed most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and some powerlines were downed onto Horseshoe Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predecessor rainfall event resulted in flash flooding across portions of the area as moisture streaming northward ahead of Tropical Storm Elsa encountered a stationary boundary draped across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged 2-4 inches across portions of Manhattan, the Bronx, and Westchester County, with lesser amounts elsewhere. The ASOS in Central Park reported 2.27 inches of rain from this event, while the New York State Mesonet station in Fordham reported 3.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes were closed on the Major Deegan Expressway (I-87) southbound in Fordham due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads remained closed in the Village of Fonda due to a combination of water and mud on the roads. Many roads reopened early Tuesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front brought a couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region, mainly along and north of Interstate 90. A few individual thunderstorms became severe across the Lake George Saratoga region during the early afternoon hours before the main line of thunderstorms progressed from west to east from the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley to the Hudson River during the late afternoon and early evening hours. There were a couple of reports of hail, but these storms brought mostly damaging winds with downed trees and power lines, a couple of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in a fire near West Glens Falls. Minor flooding occurred across southern Herkimer County, near Poland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed on Sixth Street in the Village of Scotia, flattening vehicles and damaging homes. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of Tuesday, July 6th, 2021, as an upper level disturbance tracked through the region. Several downed trees and wires resulted from the strongest storms. More than 35,000 customers lost power.||Additionally, heat indices across the Mid-Hudson valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level trough crossed the eastern Great Lakes from late morning through mid afternoon, providing weak large scale ascent.  A very warm and moist air mass was in place with surface dewpoints in the lower 70s and temperatures warming into the 80s, contributing to moderate to strong instability.  Shear was very weak, with effective deep layer shear only 15-20 knots. The weakly forced and weakly sheared environment in the presence of strong instability supported a pulse storm mode, with rapidly developing cores which briefly became severe and then quickly collapsed. Initial convective development was mainly along lake breeze boundaries, with subsequent convection developing along eastward propagating outflow boundaries that crossed most of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted on Sethland Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slight risk for severe thunderstorms was outlooked for all of our area with very unstable conditions to the south of a warm front and ahead of a shortwave impulse lifting out of Ohio. CAPE values were running 1500-2000 J/kg, and shear values were running 20-25 knots in the west to 30-40 knots in the east. Wind damage and tropical downpours ended up the main hazards as wet bulb zero heights were over 14,000 feet. Severe thunderstorms were observed across much of the area and the adjoining Great Lakes waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary just south of Lake Ontario divided a moist unstable air mass from a much cooler one. Instability was generating a CAPE of around 2000 J/Kg south of the boundary by late afternoon, but wind shear was limited with 700 hPa winds only around 20 knots. Some pulse storms began to develop by late afternoon, mainly out of our area. A convective wave approached from the Ohio Valley and passed just to our south through the evening. This resulted in rainfall estimates of 2-3 inches from the radar and flash flooding there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Route 19 between Route 16 and White Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable air mass was found across the Ohio Valley that stretched northeastward along Lake Erie and ahead of a surface cold front and mid-level shortwave trough. CAPE values were 1000 to 1200 J/kg, and bulk shear values were 35-40 knots. Storms from Ohio expanded northeast into our region through the early evening hours. A few cells became stronger over the lake and one in particular started to show deep rotation as it moved down the New York/Pennsylvania border. Secondarily, some convection crossed the Saint Lawrence River very late in the evening, yielding gusty winds on the river's northern US waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto Lakeside Drive and blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predecessor rainfall event resulted in flash flooding across portions of the area as moisture streaming northward ahead of Tropical Storm Elsa encountered a stationary boundary draped across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged 2-4 inches across portions of Manhattan, the Bronx, and Westchester County, with lesser amounts elsewhere. The ASOS in Central Park reported 2.27 inches of rain from this event, while the New York State Mesonet station in Fordham reported 3.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central Park Avenue in Yonkers was impassable due to flooding near the entrance to the old Modell's. Water was up to car doors with several vehicles stranded.

A predecessor rainfall event resulted in flash flooding across portions of the area as moisture streaming northward ahead of Tropical Storm Elsa encountered a stationary boundary draped across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged 2-4 inches across portions of Manhattan, the Bronx, and Westchester County, with lesser amounts elsewhere. The ASOS in Central Park reported 2.27 inches of rain from this event, while the New York State Mesonet station in Fordham reported 3.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All lanes were closed in both directions on the Sprain Brook Parkway in Ardsley due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary just south of Lake Ontario divided a moist unstable air mass from a much cooler one. Instability was generating a CAPE of around 2000 J/Kg south of the boundary by late afternoon, but wind shear was limited with 700 hPa winds only around 20 knots. Some pulse storms began to develop by late afternoon, mainly out of our area. A convective wave approached from the Ohio Valley and passed just to our south through the evening. This likely enhanced wind shear, especially in the Southern Tier, where numerous warnings were issued. A portion of the line that developed briefly trained in Allegany County. This resulted in rainfall estimates of 2-3 inches from the radar and flash flooding there.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto powerlines on County Road 38.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along a west-to-east oriented frontal boundary along the south shore of Lake Ontario. Instability generated CAPE values of around 1500 J/kg along and just south of the frontal boundary. Bulk shear was ample enough to support organized and even rotating updrafts. The instability was surprising considering that it was cloudy all day, but with the storms that developed, it was apparently present.||Thunderstorms developed first over the Niagara Peninsula, which then tracked into the Buffalo area. A narrow velocity couplet developed over Kenmore and Tonawanda, tracking east over Williamsville and then to Harris Hill. This feature went over the northside of the radar and made it difficult to see as it passed to our north. Another velocity signature in northeastern Erie county indicated possible rotation as well. The storms got weaker as they got into eastern Genesee County, however a ball of wind continued to track east with them. ||Another line of storms formed from Oswego to Onondaga counties. This line of storms expanded in coverage and severity while a north-south line of storms approached from Lake Ontario. These two lines merged over northern Oswego and southwest Jefferson counties, as velocity values increased near Sackets Harbor, where several trees were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Route 104 and 590. Deep water was reported on Titus Ave and Ridge Drive. Several water rescues were performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a convective complex passed south of the area. However, the northern fringe of convection associated with it clipped our area. Strong storms moved across eastern Lake Erie and persisted onto land and tracking into Chautauqua County. A brief velocity signature was present with this cell with only one report of damage received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto Route 62 between Abbey Hill Road and Little Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary during the afternoon hours of Wednesday, July 7th, 2021. Several reports of downed trees and wires resulted from the severe storms. A microburst was confirmed in Columbia and Greene Counties where an estimated 70 to 90 mph winds caused significant damage in the towns of Coxsackie and Stuyvesant. A state of emergency was declared in the town of Coxsackie as a result of the damage. ||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved into eastern New York during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th with a few reports of downed trees.||Additionally, heat indices across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in the roadway on Foordmoore Road near Routes 44 and 55.

A cold front dropped from north to south across the area. Unstable conditions were evident ahead of this, with about 2000 J/kg CAPE developing by late afternoon. Moderate mid-level shear was present with a shortwave just ahead of the front. Storms developed ahead of this frontal boundary around 3 PM and along a lake breeze boundary in Niagara County. Impressive cores aloft were quickly evident as they developed. These cells kept redeveloping back to the west, with several impressive cores aloft prompting multiple warnings. These produced numerous reports of large hail, including tennis ball sized hail Storms also trained along the boundary, producing heavy rainfall in the same location. This produced significant flash flooding in Lockport and surrounding areas. Otherwise, a line closer to the front moved across southern Ontario and across Lake Ontario and the St Lawrence River into the eastern Lake Ontario region. These storms mainly produced wind damage more than hail. Lingering more marginal storms across western New York just ahead the front caused some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms progressed from west to east across eastern New York ahead of an approaching cold front from the north during the afternoon hours on Tuesday, July 27, 2021. Damaging winds associated with these storms led to downed trees and power lines across portions of the Mohawk and Schoharie valleys and into the Capital District. Penny-size hail was also observed in Selkirk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed on Riverview Road in the Hamlet of Rexford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall set up across areas north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, July 29, 2021 north of a warm front and area of low pressure. Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell which resulted in several reports of flash flooding. In addition, a few stronger thunderstorms produced some damaging winds, downing trees and power lines. The areas hardest hit from these storms were across Saratoga and Washington counties including the towns of Argyle and Fort Edward. In this corridor, many roads were impassable or washed out from the flooding. Some roads remained closed on Friday, July 30, 2021 and beyond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moore Road remained closed in the Town of Argyle due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall set up across areas north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, July 29, 2021 north of a warm front and area of low pressure. Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell which resulted in several reports of flash flooding. In addition, a few stronger thunderstorms produced some damaging winds, downing trees and power lines. The areas hardest hit from these storms were across Saratoga and Washington counties including the towns of Argyle and Fort Edward. In this corridor, many roads were impassable or washed out from the flooding. Some roads remained closed on Friday, July 30, 2021 and beyond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Route 113 near Post Office Lane in the Town of Easton was impassable due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained over Rensselaer County during the afternoon of the 14th, resulting in a bullseye of 2-5 of rainfall over two to three hours over central and southern portions of the county. The runoff from the rainfall washed out or destroyed numerous roads and bridges, some of which would take weeks to months to reconstruct. Damages to roads and bridges totaled around $3 million, according to county officials. The Rensselaer County Executive declared a state of emergency and urged no unnecessary travel after the storms. Governor Hochul requested a Physical Disaster Declaration from the Small Business Administration, citing major damage to 18 homes and 9 businesses totaling $1.895 million, and minor damage to 281 homes and 14 businesses totaling $2.3 million. Some flash flooding extended into the northern portion of Columbia County as well. Some of the storms also produced wind damage with reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of water reaching homes and flooding basements along Berkshire Drive and Evergreen Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary during the afternoon hours of Wednesday, July 7th, 2021. Several reports of downed trees and wires resulted from the severe storms. A microburst was confirmed in Columbia and Greene Counties where an estimated 70 to 90 mph winds caused significant damage in the towns of Coxsackie and Stuyvesant. A state of emergency was declared in the town of Coxsackie as a result of the damage. ||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved into eastern New York during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th with a few reports of downed trees.||Additionally, heat indices across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon.

A state of emergency was declared in the town of Coxsackie as a result of the damage. ||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved into eastern New York during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th with a few reports of downed trees.||Additionally, heat indices across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed a microburst started in Coxsackie, and moved across the Hudson River into Stuyvesant. Damage was significant in the village of Coxsackie south and east of the Village Building on Mansion Street. Uprooted and snapped trees were widespread across the damage area. A few homes sustained damage to roofs, chimneys and siding. A state of emergency was declared for the village. The estimated path length was two miles and the estimated width was a half of a mile.

The total estimated path length in Greene and Columbia Counties was two miles and the estimated width was a half of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast across Ohio along a stationary front that stretches all the way east through western and central New York. This area of low pressure then tracked east across western New York and the Finger Lakes region. Moderate to heavy rain entered far western NY and advanced eastward across the region. One hour flash flood guidance was 1 to 2 inches across a majority of the region. This was surpassed in some locations, resulting in localized flash flooding. Otherwise, rises on rivers and creeks were the result, especially in the Buffalo area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterboro Hill Road was closed due to water flowing over the road.

Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall set up across areas north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, July 29, 2021 north of a warm front and area of low pressure. Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell which resulted in several reports of flash flooding. In addition, a few stronger thunderstorms produced some damaging winds, downing trees and power lines. The areas hardest hit from these storms were across Saratoga and Washington counties including the towns of Argyle and Fort Edward. In this corridor, many roads were impassable or washed out from the flooding. Some roads remained closed on Friday, July 30, 2021 and beyond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Fortsville Road and Clark Road was impassable due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained over Rensselaer County during the afternoon of the 14th, resulting in a bullseye of 2-5 of rainfall over two to three hours over central and southern portions of the county. The runoff from the rainfall washed out or destroyed numerous roads and bridges, some of which would take weeks to months to reconstruct. Damages to roads and bridges totaled around $3 million, according to county officials. The Rensselaer County Executive declared a state of emergency and urged no unnecessary travel after the storms. Governor Hochul requested a Physical Disaster Declaration from the Small Business Administration, citing major damage to 18 homes and 9 businesses totaling $1.895 million, and minor damage to 281 homes and 14 businesses totaling $2.3 million. Some flash flooding extended into the northern portion of Columbia County as well. Some of the storms also produced wind damage with reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 295 was reported flooded at Colane Road Intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary during the afternoon hours of Wednesday, July 7th, 2021. Several reports of downed trees and wires resulted from the severe storms. A microburst was confirmed in Columbia and Greene Counties where an estimated 70 to 90 mph winds caused significant damage in the towns of Coxsackie and Stuyvesant. A state of emergency was declared in the town of Coxsackie as a result of the damage. ||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved into eastern New York during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th with a few reports of downed trees.||Additionally, heat indices across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Pine Bush Road near Stilba Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast across Ohio along a stationary front that stretches all the way east through western and central New York. This area of low pressure then tracked east across western New York and the Finger Lakes region. Moderate to heavy rain entered far western NY and advanced eastward across the region. One hour flash flood guidance was 1 to 2 inches across a majority of the region. This was surpassed in some locations, resulting in localized flash flooding. Otherwise, rises on rivers and creeks were the result, especially in the Buffalo area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was backing up the Bemus Creek, across Bemus Point, due to the rising level of Chautauqua Lake. Voluntary evacuations of area homes was underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped from north to south across the area. Unstable conditions were evident ahead of this, with about 2000 J/kg CAPE developing by late afternoon. Moderate mid-level shear was present with a shortwave just ahead of the front. Storms developed ahead of this frontal boundary around 3 PM and along a lake breeze boundary in Niagara County. Impressive cores aloft were quickly evident as they developed. These cells kept redeveloping back to the west, with several impressive cores aloft prompting multiple warnings. These produced numerous reports of large hail, including tennis ball sized hail Storms also trained along the boundary, producing heavy rainfall in the same location. This produced significant flash flooding in Lockport and surrounding areas. Otherwise, a line closer to the front moved across southern Ontario and across Lake Ontario and the St Lawrence River into the eastern Lake Ontario region. These storms mainly produced wind damage more than hail. Lingering more marginal storms across western New York just ahead the front caused some wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced torrential rainfall across the Village of Fonda on Monday, July 19, 2021. Between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell in just a couple of hours. A small creek north of the village overflowed its banks, sending water and mud downstream into the village. Storm drains became plugged up with debris so water was unable to get through. Many roads were closed and impassable in and around the village until early Tuesday morning, July 20, 2021. ||A state of emergency was issued in the Village of Fonda as a result of the flooding.||In addition, this thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Johnstown, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of flash flooding in and around the Village of Fonda. A small creek north of Fonda overflowed its banks causing 6 to 8 inches of water and mud to flow down Broadway (Route 30A) to Main Street (Route 5), causing multiple roads to be closed. Road shoulders were washed out on Old Johnson Road. The Interstate 90 off-ramps were closed at Exit 28 in the village of Fultonville due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front brought a couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region, mainly along and north of Interstate 90. A few individual thunderstorms became severe across the Lake George Saratoga region during the early afternoon hours before the main line of thunderstorms progressed from west to east from the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley to the Hudson River during the late afternoon and early evening hours. There were a couple of reports of hail, but these storms brought mostly damaging winds with downed trees and power lines, a couple of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in a fire near West Glens Falls. Minor flooding occurred across southern Herkimer County, near Poland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near State Route 50 and King Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms resulted in severe weather and flash flooding over eastern New York and western New England Saturday-Sunday July 17-18, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9W was closed due to flooding between Glasco Turnpike and Belevedere Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front brought a couple of rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms across the region, mainly along and north of Interstate 90. A few individual thunderstorms became severe across the Lake George Saratoga region during the early afternoon hours before the main line of thunderstorms progressed from west to east from the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley to the Hudson River during the late afternoon and early evening hours. There were a couple of reports of hail, but these storms brought mostly damaging winds with downed trees and power lines, a couple of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in a fire near West Glens Falls. Minor flooding occurred across southern Herkimer County, near Poland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were downed and arcing in the area of East German Street and Lake Street in the Village of Herkimer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unnamed creek on the northern side of Fonda overflowed its banks for the second time in a week on Friday, July 23, 2021, after a couple of thunderstorms produced a quick 1 to 2 inches of rainfall in the early afternoon hours. This caused Broadway between Route 5 and Wemple Avenue to close due to an estimated 3 inches of fast-moving water and mud on the road. This event was noted less severe than on July 19 with little to no impact on area businesses. The road reopened later Friday afternoon once the waters receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadway between Route 5 and Wemple Avenue was closed due to flash flooding after an unnamed creek overflowed its banks north of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked northeast across Ohio along a stationary front that stretches all the way east through western and central New York. This area of low pressure then tracked east across western New York and the Finger Lakes region. Moderate to heavy rain entered far western NY and advanced eastward across the region. One hour flash flood guidance was 1 to 2 inches across a majority of the region. This was surpassed in some locations, resulting in localized flash flooding. Otherwise, rises on rivers and creeks were the result, especially in the Buffalo area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six hour rainfall report was received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped from north to south across the area. Unstable conditions were evident ahead of this, with about 2000 J/kg CAPE developing by late afternoon. Moderate mid-level shear was present with a shortwave just ahead of the front. Storms developed ahead of this frontal boundary around 3 PM and along a lake breeze boundary in Niagara County. Impressive cores aloft were quickly evident as they developed. These cells kept redeveloping back to the west, with several impressive cores aloft prompting multiple warnings. These produced numerous reports of large hail, including tennis ball sized hail Storms also trained along the boundary, producing heavy rainfall in the same location. This produced significant flash flooding in Lockport and surrounding areas. Otherwise, a line closer to the front moved across southern Ontario and across Lake Ontario and the St Lawrence River into the eastern Lake Ontario region. These storms mainly produced wind damage more than hail. Lingering more marginal storms across western New York just ahead the front caused some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional reports of widespread flooding were received with stranded vehicles in other portions of Lockport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa tracked across the eastern portion of the area during the morning hours of July 9, with heavy rainfall north and west of the storm's center resulting in flash flooding across portions of Long Island and the Lower Hudson Valley. Rainfall totals ranged from 1.5-4 inches, highest across Long Island. New York State Mesonet stations measured 3.27 inches in Wantagh and 3.44 inches in Stony Brook. An automated weather station measured 1.79 inches in New Rochelle. The ASOS at Islip Airport measured 2.26 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police reported that numerous roadways were flooded and impassable in Glen Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a stalled frontal boundary during the afternoon hours of Wednesday, July 7th, 2021. Several reports of downed trees and wires resulted from the severe storms. A microburst was confirmed in Columbia and Greene Counties where an estimated 70 to 90 mph winds caused significant damage in the towns of Coxsackie and Stuyvesant. A state of emergency was declared in the town of Coxsackie as a result of the damage. ||A second round of severe thunderstorms moved into eastern New York during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th with a few reports of downed trees.||Additionally, heat indices across the mid-Hudson Valley and southern Taconics exceeded 95 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A live tree around 6 inches in diameter was snapped. There was also a few large 3 - 4 inch diameter branches downed in a yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front dropped from north to south across the area. Unstable conditions were evident ahead of this, with about 2000 J/kg CAPE developing by late afternoon. Moderate mid-level shear was present with a shortwave just ahead of the front. Storms developed ahead of this frontal boundary around 3 PM and along a lake breeze boundary in Niagara County. Impressive cores aloft were quickly evident as they developed. These cells kept redeveloping back to the west, with several impressive cores aloft prompting multiple warnings. These produced numerous reports of large hail, including tennis ball sized hail Storms also trained along the boundary, producing heavy rainfall in the same location. This produced significant flash flooding in Lockport and surrounding areas. Otherwise, a line closer to the front moved across southern Ontario and across Lake Ontario and the St Lawrence River into the eastern Lake Ontario region. These storms mainly produced wind damage more than hail. Lingering more marginal storms across western New York just ahead the front caused some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tennis ball sized hail was reported in Lewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained over Rensselaer County during the afternoon of the 14th, resulting in a bullseye of 2-5 of rainfall over two to three hours over central and southern portions of the county. The runoff from the rainfall washed out or destroyed numerous roads and bridges, some of which would take weeks to months to reconstruct. Damages to roads and bridges totaled around $3 million, according to county officials. The Rensselaer County Executive declared a state of emergency and urged no unnecessary travel after the storms. Governor Hochul requested a Physical Disaster Declaration from the Small Business Administration, citing major damage to 18 homes and 9 businesses totaling $1.895 million, and minor damage to 281 homes and 14 businesses totaling $2.3 million. Some flash flooding extended into the northern portion of Columbia County as well. Some of the storms also produced wind damage with reports of trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of roads flooded or washed out in the Eagle Mills area, including Garfield Road, Creek Road, and Dater Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall set up across areas north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon and evening hours on Thursday, July 29, 2021 north of a warm front and area of low pressure. Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell which resulted in several reports of flash flooding. In addition, a few stronger thunderstorms produced some damaging winds, downing trees and power lines. The areas hardest hit from these storms were across Saratoga and Washington counties including the towns of Argyle and Fort Edward. In this corridor, many roads were impassable or washed out from the flooding. Some roads remained closed on Friday, July 30, 2021 and beyond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Basement flooding occurred along Fort Miller Road in the Town of Fort Edward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment and a passing disturbance triggered many severe storms across Central New York. Large hail and damaging winds were the hazards associated with these storms. A tree fell on an occupied vehicle, but the occupant was uninjured. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in the house catching on fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Central New York with multiple storms forming along it. Many storms became severe due to instability ahead of the front, resulting in multiple reports of trees and wires down. A mesonet stations measured a wind gust of 51 knots as a storm passed overhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment in Central New York allowed for thunderstorms, triggered by a passing warm front, to become severe with torrential rainfall. Rainfall totals of 3 to 4 inches, with locally higher amounts, were reported in 2 to 3 hours. These storms produced areas of high impact flash flooding in many parts of Broome county. Several hundred thousand dollars in damage to public infrastructure occurred, along with an additional major loss to private property in portions of the Towns of Chenango and Binghamton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were flooded all around the Belden area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred were forecast to move across the region. Out ahead of the remnants of Fred, a slow-moving frontal zone and tropical moisture spreading north ahead of Fred allowed for a pre-cursory heavy rainfall event on August 17. This set the stage for later flooding as moisture more specifically associated with Fred advanced into the area during the next two days. A large swath of moderate to heavy rain moved in on August 17 and continued through the next morning. This resulted in impressive 24 hour totals across the area with greatest amounts in Allegany, Ontario, Wayne, and Lewis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall that was reported during the entirety of the passage of the remnants of Tropical Storm Fred.

This set the stage for later flooding as moisture more specifically associated with Fred advanced into the area during the next two days. A large swath of moderate to heavy rain moved in on August 17 and continued through the next morning. This resulted in impressive 24 hour totals across the area with greatest amounts in Allegany, Ontario, Wayne, and Lewis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Route 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the Great Lakes triggered thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Unstable conditions allowed for many severe storms to develop. Many of these storms had damaging winds knocking down trees and wires. A few also produced large hail. One storm had a microburst with winds estimated to be 80 mph, which resulted in multiple trees down and some structural damage including a sign for a credit union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist tropical air was present over Central New York when a quasi-stationary warm front slowly moved through the region. These conditions provided supported for severe thunderstorms with damaging winds resulting in numerous reports of trees and wires knocked down. A wind gust of nearly 70 kts was also measured by a mesonet station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid airmass remained stagnant over Central New York on this day. A weak upper level disturbance moved through the region, triggering heavy rain producing thunderstorms. Steering winds were very weak, therefore, thunderstorms lingered over the same areas resulting in urban and small stream flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to be blocking a culvert causing the road to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment in Central New York allowed for thunderstorms, triggered by a passing warm front, to become severe with torrential rainfall. Rainfall totals of 3 to 4 inches, with locally higher amounts, were reported in 2 to 3 hours. These storms produced areas of high impact flash flooding in many parts of Broome county. Several hundred thousand dollars in damage to public infrastructure occurred, along with an additional major loss to private property in portions of the Towns of Chenango and Binghamton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert washout with road damage was reported on Leigh Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment in Central New York allowed for thunderstorms, triggered by a passing warm front, to become severe with torrential rainfall. Rainfall totals of 3 to 4 inches, with locally higher amounts, were reported in 2 to 3 hours. These storms produced areas of high impact flash flooding in many parts of Broome county. Several hundred thousand dollars in damage to public infrastructure occurred, along with an additional major loss to private property in portions of the Towns of Chenango and Binghamton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wolf Creek overflowed its banks in several areas causing widespread flooding to the area. Creek debris, tree debris, mud and silt were deposited into the large drainage pipe and surrounding catch basins, as well as the roads.  Washouts occurred along several portions of Fuller Road. The pavement from the intersection of Fuller down Grant was destroyed. Homes were flooded and evacuations took place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, coupled with an extremely warm and humid environment, produced another day of slow moving, torrential downpour producing thunderstorms over Central New York. Approximately, 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time. The Southern Tier was again hit with major impacts from flash flooding of local creeks, streams and overwhelmed storm drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were reported to be flooded and washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure meandering along a stalled frontal boundary produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. With light upper level flow, these storms were generally slow moving, resulting in flash flooding across parts of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley. Rainfall amounts in the heaviest storms ranged from around 1 to 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water was reported up to the bumpers of cars in Jamaica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the Great Lakes triggered thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Unstable conditions allowed for many severe storms to develop. Many of these storms had damaging winds knocking down trees and wires. A few also produced large hail. One storm had a microburst with winds estimated to be 80 mph, which resulted in multiple trees down and some structural damage including a sign for a credit union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a vehicle, resulting in the vehicle catching on fire. Trees and utility lines were also knocked down in a separate area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, coupled with an extremely warm and humid environment, produced another day of slow moving, torrential downpour producing thunderstorms over Central New York. Approximately, 1 to 3 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time. The Southern Tier was again hit with major impacts from flash flooding of local creeks, streams and overwhelmed storm drainage systems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding of streets and poor drainage areas within the Village of Hancock. A State of Emergency was declared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, humid conditions supported a quickly destabilizing atmosphere during the afternoon. A line of thunderstorms developed and passed through Central New York. Damaging winds were associated with the northern portion of the line of storms. A tornado also developed in Oneida County, tearing off roofs on several houses in Westernville as well as damaging a barn and nearby crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees onto wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predecessor rainfall event resulted in flash flooding across portions of the area as moisture streaming northward ahead of Tropical Storm Elsa encountered a stationary boundary draped across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged 2-4 inches across portions of Manhattan, the Bronx, and Westchester County, with lesser amounts elsewhere. The ASOS in Central Park reported 2.27 inches of rain from this event, while the New York State Mesonet station in Fordham reported 3.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported up to the top of a car tire on the corner of Nagle Avenue and Dyckman Street due to flooding in Washington Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough passed over Central New York, triggering scattered thunderstorms. The environment was unstable enough for a couple of storms to produce damaging winds. Trees and utility wires were knocked down as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wires on Whiting Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fred were forecast to move across the region. Out ahead of the remnants of Fred, a slow-moving frontal zone and tropical moisture spreading north ahead of Fred allowed for a pre-cursory heavy rainfall event on August 17. This set the stage for later flooding as moisture more specifically associated with Fred advanced into the area during the next two days. A large swath of moderate to heavy rain moved in on August 17 and continued through the next morning. This resulted in impressive 24 hour totals across the area with greatest amounts in Allegany, Ontario, Wayne, and Lewis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall that was reported during the entirety of the passage of the remnants of Tropical Storm Fred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A predecessor rainfall event resulted in flash flooding across portions of the area as moisture streaming northward ahead of Tropical Storm Elsa encountered a stationary boundary draped across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged 2-4 inches across portions of Manhattan, the Bronx, and Westchester County, with lesser amounts elsewhere. The ASOS in Central Park reported 2.27 inches of rain from this event, while the New York State Mesonet station in Fordham reported 3.55 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a dozen motorists were rescued by the NYPD on the Major Deegan Expressway at W. 179th Street in Morris Heights due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southbound Hutchinson River Parkway was closed due to flooding at Exit 13 in White Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, humid conditions supported a quickly destabilizing atmosphere during the afternoon. A line of thunderstorms developed and passed through Central New York. Damaging winds were associated with the northern portion of the line of storms. A tornado also developed in Oneida County, tearing off roofs on several houses in Westernville as well as damaging a barn and nearby crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure meandering along a stalled frontal boundary produced scattered showers and thunderstorms across the region. With light upper level flow, these storms were generally slow moving, resulting in flash flooding across parts of New York City and the Lower Hudson Valley. Rainfall amounts in the heaviest storms ranged from around 1 to 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitestone Expressway was closed at 22nd Road in Flushing due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and early evening of the 6th across portions of Central New York. Several of these storms became severe with damaging winds. Mainly tree and powerline damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds brought down a tree and wires, causing a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment and a passing disturbance triggered many severe storms across Central New York. Large hail and damaging winds were the hazards associated with these storms. A tree fell on an occupied vehicle, but the occupant was uninjured. Lightning also struck a house, resulting in the house catching on fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a vehicle in Delta Lake State Park. No injuries were reported as the woman in vehicle refused treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved through Central New York, triggering scattered thunderstorms. Several storms produced torrential rainfall which led to flash flooding. Between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell over the southern part of Broome county in approximately a 3 to 4 hour time span.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roberts and Pierce Creek Roads were flooded and covered with debris. Roads were impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system over the Great Lakes triggered thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening across Central New York. Unstable conditions allowed for many severe storms to develop. Many of these storms had damaging winds knocking down trees and wires. A few also produced large hail. One storm had a microburst with winds estimated to be 80 mph, which resulted in multiple trees down and some structural damage including a sign for a credit union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto vehicle. Trees and utility wires were also knocked down in a separate location.
